A Causal Framework for Regimen Forgiveness in HIV Treatment

Author

Andrew Mertens

Published

March 24, 2026

Abstract

Background. The forgiveness of an antiretroviral (ART) regimen, its capacity to maintain viral suppression despite imperfect adherence, varies across drug classes and has direct implications for regimen selection in both clinical practice and real-world evidence studies. A key question in HIV treatment research is at what level of suboptimal adherence each regimen still maintains functional suppression. Conventional adherence-stratified analyses produce biased estimates of this regimen-specific adherence–response relationship because time-varying clinical status (e.g., viral load trajectory, tolerability, depression) simultaneously influences future adherence and future virologic outcomes.

Methods. We formalize regimen forgiveness as a regimen-specific causal adherence–response curve: the expected probability of virologic failure under hypothetical interventions that sustain adherence at specified levels over time. We adopt a longitudinal causal inference framework with time-varying adherence and time-dependent confounders affected by prior adherence, and define the target parameter using counterfactual outcomes under static adherence regimes. Identification relies on standard assumptions of consistency, sequential exchangeability, and positivity, with explicit consideration of treatment–confounder feedback. We implement this framework in a simulated cohort with known data-generating mechanisms, enabling direct evaluation against oracle counterfactual risks. Estimation proceeds using g-methods appropriate for longitudinal interventions, including longitudinal targeted minimum loss-based estimation (LTMLE) for static adherence regimes and modified treatment policies (LMTP) for feasible adherence interventions, with additional extensions for delivery-system comparisons and complex data structures described in the appendix. Naive adherence-stratified analyses are included as a descriptive benchmark to illustrate bias under time-dependent confounding. We assess estimator performance and inferential properties using a 1,763-replication Monte Carlo study, evaluating bias, variance calibration, and confidence interval coverage for both regime-specific risks and derived contrasts.

Results. Naive adherence-stratified analyses produce systematically biased estimates of the adherence–response relationship: treatment–confounder feedback inflates the apparent effect of adherence and distorts relative forgiveness between regimens. Under correct longitudinal adjustment, the estimated regime-specific counterfactual risks recover the true forgiveness curve where data support is adequate — with both drugs performing similarly at high adherence and diverging as adherence declines — though at weakly-supported adherence extremes, positivity constraints limit accuracy even for targeted estimators. Static-regime estimation is sensitive to positivity: when adherence paths are weakly supported, estimates exhibit increased variance and reliance on model extrapolation. Feasible policy interventions defined via modified treatment policies yield more stable and well-calibrated estimates by targeting adherence shifts that remain within the support of the observed data. Across Monte Carlo replications, targeted estimators substantially reduce confounding bias relative to naive estimation at all adherence levels, including low adherence where naive estimates show the greatest attenuation; however, positivity constraints inflate LTMLE’s variance at low adherence, yielding conservative coverage with low power despite near-zero point-estimate bias. LMTP-static suffers density-ratio collapse under weak positivity, yielding anti-conservative inference with nominal 95% CIs achieving as little as 14% empirical coverage. Risk ratios are unstable when risks approach the boundary near zero; the risk difference is the preferred primary contrast for forgiveness comparisons.

Conclusions. Regimen forgiveness is a causal adherence–response function defined by counterfactual outcomes under sustained adherence interventions, not an observable feature of adherence-stratified data. Because adherence and clinical status evolve jointly over time, valid estimation requires longitudinal g-methods that address treatment–confounder feedback and respect positivity. This tutorial provides a practical framework for defining, estimating, and interpreting forgiveness curves in real-world HIV studies, with extensions illustrating how the same methods apply to alternative adherence processes such as long-acting injectable therapies.

1 Introduction

1.1 What is regimen forgiveness?

Antiretroviral therapy (ART) suppresses HIV viral replication when drug concentrations remain above the therapeutic threshold. For daily oral regimens, this requires consistent pill-taking, what the medication-adherence literature terms execution (Vrijens et al. 2012). In practice, imperfect adherence is common. It may arise because of adverse effects, depression, unstable housing, or other competing life demands. Different ART drug classes tolerate missed doses differently: a patient with otherwise stable HIV who misses several doses over a month may remain suppressed on one regimen but experience viral rebound on another with less pharmacologic forgiveness. Quantifying this tolerance is essential for guiding regimen selection, yet it raises a fundamentally causal question that standard analytic approaches cannot answer.

The forgiveness of an ART regimen is its ability to maintain viral suppression despite imperfect adherence. A more forgiving regimen has a flatter adherence–risk curve: the risk of virologic failure rises slowly as adherence declines, whereas a less forgiving regimen’s risk escalates sharply. Forgiveness is clinically meaningful because ART regimens differ in half-life, pharmacokinetic buffering, resistance barrier, and vulnerability to missed doses. A forgiveness analysis therefore asks not only whether adherence matters, but how sharply outcome risk changes as adherence declines for a given regimen.

Forgiveness is not an intrinsic property of a drug’s pharmacology alone. It depends on how the drug is used in real life—how regularly patients take it, how their health evolves, and how consistent their care is. Stable care refers to settings with reliable medication access, regular follow-up, and relatively steady clinical status, whereas adherence is harder to maintain when patients face disruptions such as side effects, mental health challenges, or gaps in care. As a result, two patients with the same measured adherence may not have the same risk: missed doses may have little effect in a stable context but lead to failure when health is worsening or care is inconsistent. In causal terms, the forgiveness curve depends on the joint distribution of adherence and time-varying clinical factors in the population, and therefore represents a population-specific causal relationship under defined intervention conditions, not a fixed property of the drug.

Forgiveness is not a fixed pharmacologic property of a drug alone, but a counterfactual property of the joint treatment–adherence system. It depends on regimen pharmacology, adherence behavior, time-varying clinical status, outcome definition, and the monitoring and switching processes through which virologic failure is detected and acted upon.

This has direct clinical relevance. This distinction is not merely methodological: biased estimates of forgiveness can lead to incorrect conclusions about regimen robustness in populations with imperfect adherence. When two regimens achieve similar efficacy at high adherence, the more forgiving option is preferred for populations where adherence barriers are anticipated, such as patients with depression, substance use, or housing instability (Bangsberg 2006; Paterson et al. 2000). This is particularly relevant when selecting between regimens with similar efficacy under trial conditions but differing tolerance to imperfect adherence in practice.

In real-world evidence (RWE) studies of HIV treatment, two broad causal questions arise:

  1. Comparative effectiveness: Is ART regimen 1 superior to comparator regimens in real-world settings on outcomes such as viral suppression or ART discontinuation?
  2. Adherence forgiveness: At what level of suboptimal adherence does each regimen still maintain functional viral suppression?

Empirically, forgiveness differs across drug classes. Earlier HIV regimens required near-perfect adherence to maintain viral suppression (Paterson et al. 2000), whereas later classes became more tolerant of missed doses (Bangsberg 2006). Modern regimens, particularly those based on integrase inhibitors, tend to be more forgiving, maintaining suppression even with some lapses in adherence. Long-acting injectable therapies represent a different approach, providing sustained drug levels over time rather than relying on daily pill-taking. For long-acting injectable therapies, the relevant adherence process shifts from daily pill-taking to visit attendance and timely re-dosing, and forgiveness reflects pharmacologic persistence as well as delivery-system reliability. Current clinical interest focuses on comparing these newer regimens, such as two- versus three-drug combinations and oral versus injectable therapies, where even modest differences in forgiveness may influence treatment choice for patients with anticipated adherence challenges.

1.2 Adherence measurement in real-world data

In real-world pharmacoepidemiology studies of HIV treatment, adherence is typically operationalised using the proportion of days covered (PDC) derived from pharmacy claims data, calculated over rolling windows (i.e. 90 days) preceding viral load measurements. PDC represents the fraction of days in a period for which a patient had medication supply on hand, based on fill dates and days’ supply. PDC is then often categorized into ordered bins (e.g., 0%, 1–65%, 65–75%, 75–85%, ≥85%), then adherence is treated as a time-varying exposure that changes across successive measurement windows. The simulated adherence variables in this tutorial (\(Z_t \in \{1, 2, 3\}\) representing low, medium, and high adherence at each 90-day block) are a simplified abstraction of this measurement process. They capture the key structural features, time-varying, ordered, and confounded by clinical status, without modelling the full complexity of pharmacy claims processing. Pharmacy refill measures such as PDC capture medication availability rather than confirmed ingestion. Similarly, viral rebound is only observed at intermittent monitoring times, so the precise timing of loss of viral suppression is latent between measurements.

1.3 Relationship to prior work

In operational real-world evidence (RWE) studies of HIV treatment, adherence–outcome relationships are typically analysed using repeated-measures regression (e.g., GEE), inverse probability weighting for baseline confounding, or time-to-event models with time-updated adherence. These approaches serve legitimate descriptive and associational purposes and remain valuable for characterising adherence patterns and their correlates. Conventional regression approaches may describe conditional or associational adherence-outcome relationships, but they do not generally target intervention-defined counterfactual risks under treatment-confounder feedback without additional assumptions.

Prior HIV adherence research has included both associational analyses and causal approaches using marginal structural models and related g-methods to estimate the effects of adherence on virologic outcomes. However, these studies have typically focused on contrasts between a limited number of adherence levels or summary measures of adherence, rather than characterizing the full adherence–response relationship across its range. The key contribution of this work is to formalize regimen forgiveness as a causal adherence–response curve defined by counterfactual outcomes under sustained adherence interventions, and to demonstrate how this curve can be estimated using longitudinal g-methods. By reframing adherence effects as a continuous, regimen-specific function and explicitly addressing positivity and support limitations, this framework extends prior work toward a more clinically interpretable and intervention-relevant characterization of adherence tolerance.

Earlier work on adherence and virologic failure using marginal structural models and targeted learning, notably Rosenblum and Laan (2010) and Petersen et al. (2014), established intervention-based summaries of adherence effects as a principled alternative to crude adherence thresholds. This tutorial extends that perspective to forgiveness curves, feasible policy interventions, and delivery-system comparisons. The methodological foundations include g-methods for time-varying treatments (Robins 1986; Robins, Hernán, and Brumback 2000) and LTMLE as a doubly robust alternative (Laan and Rubin 2006; Laan and Gruber 2012). Díaz, Williams, et al. (2021) and Díaz et al. (2023) introduced modified treatment policies (implemented in the lmtp R package, Williams and Díaz (2023)) that allow for defining interventions relative to the observed adherence level, avoiding the positivity violations that arise with static regimes. Where prior MSM-based analyses estimate average or contrast-based effects of adherence, this framework estimates the full adherence–response function that defines regimen tolerance.

These distinctions have direct clinical implications. When two regimens achieve similar virologic suppression under high adherence, differences in forgiveness may guide regimen selection for patients with anticipated adherence challenges — such as those with unstable housing, mental health conditions, or substance use. More forgiving regimens may reduce the risk of virologic rebound under imperfect adherence and may also influence the accumulation of resistance-associated mutations, particularly in settings of intermittent adherence. At a population level, understanding regimen-specific forgiveness may inform treatment guidelines and public health strategies by identifying regimens that maintain suppression under realistic adherence patterns.

Our contribution is to frame forgiveness as a regimen-specific causal adherence–response curve, demonstrate why naive approaches fail to estimate the causal quantity of interest using a transparent simulated cohort, and provide a complete implementation guide with LTMLE for static adherence regimes and LMTP for feasible shift policies. Although the primary worked example uses daily oral ART, the framework applies to any treatment modality where effectiveness depends on time-varying adherence; we illustrate this generality with an extension to long-acting injectable ART (Section 13.9.1). The scientific advance here is not the general idea that adherence matters, nor the use of g-methods in HIV alone, but the formalization of regimen forgiveness as a regimen-specific causal adherence–response function that can be estimated, compared, and interpreted under realistic support constraints.

2 Causal Roadmap Applied to ART Forgiveness

To formalize these ideas, we now adopt the causal roadmap (Dang et al. 2023), moving from (1) the scientific question and the accompanying causal estimand, to (2) the identification assumptions, then (3) the statistical estimand, and finally (4) the statistical estimation strategy.

2.1 The causal question

This tutorial focuses on estimating how virologic outcomes would change under different hypothetical adherence patterns to ART. We define the causal estimand as the expected probability of virologic failure if adherence were maintained at a specified level over time. The forgiveness curve then summarises this outcome across a range of adherence levels, showing how far adherence can decline before regimen effectiveness meaningfully deteriorates.

Formally, the estimand is:

\[ \psi(a_0, z) = E\!\left[Y_K^{a_0, \bar{z}}\right] \]

where \(Y_K^{a_0, \bar{z}}\) is the outcome that would be observed if drug assignment were set to \(a_0\) and adherence were maintained at level \(z\) across all \(K\) time blocks. The forgiveness curve traces \(\psi(a_0, z)\) as a function of \(z\) for each regimen \(a_0\). In practical terms, this estimand represents the probability of virologic failure if adherence were maintained at a specified level over time.

In routine clinical care, adherence is not randomly assigned. It is influenced by prior viral load, tolerability, comorbidities, care engagement, and other patient factors that also affect future virologic outcomes. As a result, descriptive comparisons across observed adherence strata do not generally identify the causal effect of adherence. The gap between the causal estimand and the observed association can be substantial, with a likely direction of bias (see Section 3). This setting therefore illustrates the core challenges of longitudinal causal inference, including time-varying adherence and confounders affected by prior adherence.

Three distinct questions

These three questions correspond to related causal estimands, all defined as projections or contrasts of the same underlying counterfactual risk \(\psi(a_0, z)\).

  1. Regimen comparison asks whether one ART regimen leads to better virologic outcomes than another at a given adherence level. This corresponds to contrasts of the form:

\[ \text{RD}(z) = \psi(1, z) - \psi(0, z), \quad \text{RR}(z) = \frac{\psi(1, z)}{\psi(0, z)}. \]

Here, \(z\) may represent a fixed adherence level (e.g., sustained high adherence) or an intervention-defined adherence distribution.

  1. Adherence-effect estimation asks how outcomes would change under different adherence levels within a regimen. This corresponds to contrasts such as:

\[ \psi(a_0, z_1) - \psi(a_0, z_2), \]

comparing higher versus lower adherence for a fixed regimen.

  1. Forgiveness-curve estimation asks how outcome risk varies across the full range of adherence levels. This is given directly by the function \(z \mapsto \psi(a_0, z)\), which characterises how tolerant a regimen is to missed doses.

All three questions are therefore functions of the same underlying estimand \(\psi(a_0, z)\), differing only in how it is summarized or contrasted. A forgiveness curve contains multiple adherence-effect contrasts as special cases, but provides a more complete summary of how outcome risk changes across adherence levels.

In addition to characterizing regimen forgiveness, an applied comparative question is whether one regimen is superior to another in real-world use. This comparison can be defined in at least two ways: first, under each regimen’s observed adherence pattern, yielding a real-world effectiveness contrast; and second, under a common adherence intervention or common adherence distribution, yielding a contrast that isolates differences in regimen-specific outcome risk apart from adherence differences. These estimands answer different scientific questions and should not be conflated. The present framework is naturally suited to both formulations, although the main text continues to emphasize the forgiveness estimand.

2.2 Causal estimands

Building on the causal question above, we define the primary estimand and related contrasts of interest.

The static-regime risk \(\psi(a_0, z)\) is the primary estimand, defining the forgiveness curve. Regimen comparisons and adherence-effect contrasts are derived from this quantity, while the LMTP shift-policy estimand \(\psi^d\) targets a complementary, more feasible intervention when data support (positivity) is limited (see Section 13.4 for full positivity diagnostics). Static adherence regimes define a clear causal estimand but may not correspond to feasible real-world interventions, because they require adherence to be set deterministically without specifying the mechanism by which that level is achieved. For a mapping of this estimand to the ICH E9(R1) estimand framework, see Section 13.1.

A regimen comparison under the natural course asks how virologic risk would differ if patients initiated regimen A versus regimen B while adherence evolved as it naturally does under each regimen. A controlled-adherence comparison instead asks how virologic risk would differ if both regimens were evaluated under the same adherence distribution or the same adherence intervention. The latter estimand is especially useful when the goal is to separate biologic or pharmacologic regimen differences from differences operating through adherence behavior. These contrasts are related to, but distinct from, the regimen-specific adherence-response function used to characterize forgiveness.

2.3 Observed data structure

For a cohort of \(n\) patients followed over \(K = 4\) blocks of 90 days (~1 year), the observed data for patient \(i\) is:

\[O_i = (W_i, A_{0,i}, L_{1,i}, Z_{1,i}, Y_{1,i}, \ldots, L_{K,i}, Z_{K,i}, Y_{K,i})\]

where:

Node Description
\(W\) Baseline covariates: age (continuous), severity/low CD4 (binary), depression (binary), prior treatment instability (binary)
\(A_0\) Regimen assignment: 1 = Drug A (more forgiving), 0 = Drug B (less forgiving). Randomised 1:1.
\(L_t\) Time-varying health status at block \(t\) (continuous). Depends on \(L_{t-1}\), \(Z_{t-1}\), and \(A_0\).
\(Z_t\) Adherence at block \(t\): categorical \(\{1 = \text{low}, 2 = \text{medium}, 3 = \text{high}\}\). Depends on \(L_t\), \(W\), and \(Z_{t-1}\).
\(Y_t\) Virologic failure at block \(t\) (binary, absorbing). Depends on \(Z_t\), \(L_t\), \(A_0\), \(W\), and crucially the \(A_0 \times Z_t\) interaction.

The causal ordering within each block is \(L_t \to Z_t \to Y_t\), with \(L_t\) depending on prior \(Z_{t-1}\) (treatment-confounder feedback).

2.4 Structural causal model

The causal process in plain language

Before introducing notation, it helps to walk through the data-generating story as a clinician would describe it. A patient initiates ART on one of two regimens. Over the next year, their adherence evolves across repeated 90-day periods, sometimes high, sometimes low, driven by tolerability, life circumstances, and clinical status. At each period, the patient’s current clinical state (viral load trajectory, side effects, comorbidity burden) depends in part on how well they adhered in the previous period: good adherence improves health, while poor adherence may lead to rising viral load or worsening symptoms. That clinical state then shapes their adherence in the current period (a patient feeling unwell may miss doses), and their current adherence together with their clinical state determines the risk of virologic failure. The causal chain runs: regimen \(\to\) adherence behaviour \(\to\) pharmacologic exposure \(\to\) virologic outcome, with clinical status feeding back into adherence at each time point.

This feedback loop is what makes the forgiveness problem fundamentally different from a single-time-point treatment effect, and it is the reason that standard regression, even with careful covariate adjustment, cannot recover the causal adherence–response curve. The core difficulty is the same confounding problem that arises with propensity-score-adjusted analyses of a single treatment decision, but repeated at every time point, with the added complication that the confounders themselves are affected by prior treatment.

The DAG below shows this structure for two time blocks:

Formal structural equations

The structural causal model (SCM) formalises this causal process as a system of equations. Each variable is written as a deterministic function of its causal parents plus an unobserved error term \(U\):

\[\begin{align} W &= f_W(U_W) \\ A_0 &= f_{A_0}(W, U_{A_0}) \\ L_t &= f_{L_t}(L_{t-1}, Z_{t-1}, A_0, U_{L_t}) & \text{for } t = 1, \ldots, K \\ Z_t &= f_{Z_t}(L_t, Z_{t-1}, W, U_{Z_t}) & \text{for } t = 1, \ldots, K \\ Y_t &= f_{Y_t}(Z_t, L_t, A_0, W, Y_{t-1}, U_{Y_t}) & \text{for } t = 1, \ldots, K \end{align}\]

The SCM encodes the same story as the DAG: \(L_t\) depends on prior adherence \(Z_{t-1}\) (adherence affects health) and \(Z_t\) depends on current \(L_t\) (health affects adherence). This treatment-confounder feedback is why standard regression fails. The functions \(f\) are left unspecified, the SCM makes no parametric assumptions about functional forms, only about which variables cause which. The simulation DGP in Section 4 provides one concrete implementation of these structural equations.

2.5 Identification

To move from the causal estimand (what would happen under a hypothetical adherence pattern) to a quantity we can estimate from observed data, we need three assumptions. In plain language: (1) the outcome we observe for a patient who happens to follow a given adherence pattern is the same outcome they would have had if we had assigned that pattern (Consistency); (2) at each time point, we have measured enough of the patient’s clinical history that, conditional on those measurements, adherence is unrelated to unmeasured risk factors (Sequential exchangeability); and (3) every adherence level we want to evaluate is actually observed with positive probability in the data (positivity — meaning the data contain sufficient patients with each adherence pattern to estimate those counterfactual risks reliably). See Section 13.2 for a formal treatment of these three assumptions.

2.6 From causal estimand to statistical target parameter

The observed data for each patient, \(O_i = (W_i, A_{0,i}, \bar{L}_i, \bar{Z}_i, \bar{Y}_i)\), are drawn independently from an unknown distribution \(P_0\). The scientific question, how does virologic failure risk change across adherence levels for each regimen?, is encoded by the causal estimand \(\psi(a_0, z) = E[Y_K^{a_0, \bar{z}}]\). This quantity is defined through the SCM and refers to a counterfactual world; it is not directly a function of \(P_0\).

Under the identification assumptions (consistency, sequential exchangeability, and positivity), the causal estimand equals a statistical target parameter, a functional of the observed-data distribution:

\[\Psi_{a_0,z}(P_0) = \sum_{\bar{l}} \prod_{t=1}^{K} P_0(Y_t = 0 \mid \bar{z}_t, \bar{l}_t, a_0, w) \cdot P_0(l_t \mid \bar{z}_{t-1}, \bar{l}_{t-1}, a_0, w)\]

This is the g-computation functional: a mapping from \(P_0\) to a real number, expressed entirely in terms of conditional distributions that can be estimated from data. Once identification holds, the problem becomes purely statistical: we need to estimate \(\Psi(P_0)\), not the counterfactual directly.

Longitudinal Targeted Maximum Likelihood Estimation (implemented in either the ltmle or lmtp package) is a targeted estimator of \(\Psi(P_0)\). It constructs an estimate \(\hat{P}_n\) of the relevant parts of \(P_0\) (outcome regressions and treatment/adherence mechanisms) through a sequence of models over time, and then evaluates the functional \(\Psi(\hat{P}_n)\). The “targeting” step updates the initial estimate to reduce bias for \(\Psi\) specifically, rather than for the full distribution \(P_0\). It is doubly robust: \(\Psi(\hat{P}_n)\) is consistent for \(\Psi(P_0)\) if either the outcome model or the treatment model is correctly specified (Bang and Robins 2005; Laan and Gruber 2012; Díaz et al. 2023). Influence-curve-based standard errors and confidence intervals follow from the efficient influence function of \(\Psi\) at \(P_0\).

3 Why Single-time Point Adherence-Stratified Analyses Fail

Before implementing causal methods, it is instructive to understand why the standard epidemiologic approach — stratifying or adjusting for observed adherence — produces biased estimates of the forgiveness curve. The core issue is that adherence is time-varying, clinical status influences future adherence, and clinical status also predicts future virologic outcomes. This creates a feedback structure that standard regression cannot handle correctly, and it is the primary motivation for using g-methods (marginal structural models, g-computation, TMLE, or LMTP) in adherence analyses.

3.1 The treatment-confounder feedback loop

Naive adherence-stratified analyses fail because adherence and clinical status evolve jointly over time through a feedback process. First, worsening clinical status — such as rising viral load or treatment side effects — can reduce a patient’s ability to maintain adherence. Second, reduced adherence leads to poorer virologic control and further deterioration in clinical status. Third, this updated clinical status continues to influence subsequent adherence behavior. As a result, observed adherence at any time point reflects both prior adherence and prior health. Conditioning on observed adherence therefore induces selection bias, because adherence acts as a post-treatment variable affected by prior outcomes.

Observed adherence strata reflect realized behavior under evolving clinical conditions, whereas the target estimand concerns counterfactual outcomes under intervention-defined adherence patterns.

The fundamental problem is that adherence after baseline is a post-treatment variable: it is influenced by prior health status, prior adherence, and the treatment itself. Those same prior factors also affect future virologic outcomes. For concrete example, consider that a patient who develops nausea or early virologic rebound may miss doses during the next 90-day period. If we then compare high-adherence and low-adherence periods without properly accounting for this evolving clinical history, we risk attributing to adherence differences that are partly due to worsening health status.

Comparing patients who happened to achieve high versus low adherence therefore does not recover the causal forgiveness curve, because the comparison is confounded by the clinical trajectory that led to each adherence pattern. This is the same logic that makes per-protocol analyses in randomised trials vulnerable to bias when protocol deviations are informative. More specifically, standard regression can be biased because some clinical variables both affect future adherence and predict future virologic outcomes. For example, a patient with rising viral load, side effects, depression, or worsening comorbidity burden may become less adherent in the next interval, and those same factors also increase the risk of future virologic failure. Because these variables change over time and may themselves be affected by prior adherence, they are time-dependent confounders.

In the structural model above, health status \(L_t\) is simultaneously:

  1. A confounder of the \(Z_t \to Y_t\) relationship (healthier patients have lower failure risk regardless of adherence), and
  2. A consequence of prior adherence \(Z_{t-1}\) (better adherence improves health via viral suppression).

This creates a feedback loop: \(Z_{t-1} \to L_t \to Z_t \to Y_t\). Standard regression conditions on \(L_t\) to adjust for confounding, but this also blocks the causal pathway from \(Z_{t-1}\) through \(L_t\) to \(Y_t\). Conversely, not conditioning on \(L_t\) leaves the confounder uncontrolled. Simply including time-varying clinical status in a standard outcome regression is not sufficient when those covariates are themselves affected by prior adherence. Neither approach identifies the causal effect of adherence (Hernán and Robins 2020, Ch. 21). This is the central reason that longitudinal causal methods such as marginal structural models and targeted learning approaches are needed: they are designed for settings in which adherence changes over time and is entangled with evolving patient status.

3.2 The direction of bias

The bias from naive adherence-stratified analysis has a predictable direction:

  • High-adherence stratum: Enriched for healthier patients (good health enables adherence). The observed risk is lower than the causal risk because the stratum selects for patients who would have had low failure risk regardless of adherence.
  • Low-adherence stratum: Enriched for sicker patients (illness disrupts adherence). The observed risk is higher than the causal risk because the stratum selects for patients with inherently worse prognosis.

The net effect is that naive stratification overestimates the risk difference between high and low adherence, it makes adherence appear more important than it causally is. For forgiveness curves, this means the naive approach overestimates the slope (making both drugs appear less forgiving) and can distort the relative forgiveness of Drug A versus Drug B.

3.3 What g-methods do differently

The estimators in this tutorial are longitudinal extensions of propensity-score-based confounding adjustment, adapted to settings where both adherence and confounders evolve over time.

The three main estimation strategies can be understood in practical terms before turning to technical details:

  • G-computation estimates the virologic outcome each patient would be expected to have under a specified adherence pattern, then averages those predicted outcomes across the study population (g-computation is consistent under a correctly specified outcome model but is not doubly robust, it does not provide the second chance of consistency that LTMLE and LMTP offer through their treatment model).
  • Inverse probability weighting (IPW) assigns larger weights to patients whose observed adherence pattern was less likely given their measured clinical history, and smaller weights to patients whose adherence was more expected. The weighted data approximate a study in which adherence was less confounded by measured covariates.
  • Doubly robust approaches, like longitudinal targeted maximum likelihood estimation, combine an outcome model with a treatment model so that estimation remains consistent if either model is correctly specified, and inference is better aligned with the causal estimand than either approach alone.

Specifically, the ltmle R package uses sequential regression to predict outcomes at each time point, then updates those predictions using the estimated adherence probabilities in a step called “targeting.” This targeting step directly optimises the estimate for the causal quantity of interest, reducing bias while preserving valid standard errors (Lendle et al. 2017). The LMTP (longitudinal modified treatment policy) package uses density-ratio weighting that compares the probability of the shifted adherence to the observed adherence, sidestepping the need to enumerate all confounder levels. This is particularly useful when adherence is continuous or has many levels (Díaz et al. 2023; Williams and Díaz 2023). Both methods respect the time-ordering of the data and handle the treatment-confounder feedback correctly.

4 Simulated Oral-ART Cohort

4.1 Data-generating process

We simulate a cohort of 20,000 patients randomised 1:1 to Drug A (more forgiving, e.g., an INSTI-based regimen) or Drug B (less forgiving, e.g., a PI-based regimen), followed over \(K = 4\) blocks of 90 days (~1 year). Drug assignment is randomised, which isolates longitudinal confounding from baseline selection confounding and ensures the observed drug comparison is unconfounded at baseline. The primary simulation assumes complete outcome ascertainment: every patient’s virologic failure status is observed at each block. Real applications require handling informative viral-load monitoring (i.e., patients are tested more often when clinicians suspect non-adherence), and an extension discussing this complication is provided in Section 13.10.2.

In real-world HIV studies, adherence is often approximated using pharmacy refill data, such as proportion of days covered (PDC), over clinically meaningful intervals of 30, 60, or 90 days. This is a practical proxy for medication availability, although it does not guarantee actual ingestion of the medication. For long-acting injectable regimens, adherence is instead reflected in the timing and regularity of clinic visits for scheduled injections, with missed or delayed visits leading to gaps in pharmacologic coverage. Viral load is monitored intermittently, often every few months in stable patients, more frequently when adherence or treatment response is uncertain, so observational data do not directly reveal the exact timing of virologic rebound after missed doses. Our simulation abstracts these features into discrete 90-day blocks with categorical adherence and binary failure outcomes, but the causal structure is intended to mirror the real-world data-generating process.

Baseline covariates include age in years (generated as mean 0, SD 1 on a standardized scale; the underlying raw-scale distribution approximates mean ~45 years, SD ~12), disease severity (low CD4, 30% prevalence), depression (25%), and prior treatment instability (20%). The time-varying health confounder \(L_t\) is continuous on approximately a standard normal scale, with AR(1) persistence of 0.50. The forgiveness mechanism is encoded via interaction terms in the failure hazard: both drugs have the same baseline failure probability at high adherence (\(Z = 3\)), and Drug B incurs larger penalties for low (\(Z = 1\)) and moderate (\(Z = 2\)) adherence, while Drug A has smaller penalties (Y_beta_forgive_low = -0.38 and Y_beta_forgive_med = -0.20), meaning it “forgives” missed doses more. In addition, Drug B causes modestly worse adherence due to more frequent side effects (Z_beta_A0 = 0.40), creating a drug \(\to\) adherence pathway: Drug A patients are more likely to maintain medium or high adherence, while Drug B patients drift toward lower adherence categories. This means the total risk difference between drugs reflects both the pharmacologic forgiveness mechanism (Drug B penalises missed doses more) and an adherence-mediated pathway (Drug B’s side effects cause more missed doses).

The DGP uses moderate treatment-confounder feedback (L_beta_Z = 0.30, Z_beta_L = 0.80, Y_beta_L = -0.80): better adherence moderately improves health (\(\beta_{L \leftarrow Z} = 0.30\)), better health strongly drives higher adherence (\(\beta_{Z \leftarrow L} = 0.80\)), and better health substantially reduces failure risk (\(\beta_{Y \leftarrow L} = -0.80\), corresponding to an OR of approximately 2.2 per standard deviation of \(L_t\)). The \(L_t\) AR(1) process driven by past \(Z\) values means that adherence history is implicitly captured through \(L_t\); removing the explicit \(\bar{Z}\) term (\(\beta_{Y \leftarrow \bar{Z}} = 0\)) reflects that history is encoded through \(L_t\) rather than a separate running mean. This feedback structure produces visible bias in naive adherence-stratified analyses, illustrating the “healthy adherer” effect, while remaining within a clinically plausible parameter range.

Calibration note. The DGP was tuned to be more consistent with contemporary HIV care, with high-adherence failure rates in a plausible clinical range. Under forced high adherence (\(Z = 3\)), cumulative 48-week failure risks are approximately 5% for Drug A and 6% for Drug B. Under forced low adherence (\(Z = 1\)), risks rise to approximately 18% (Drug A) and 29% (Drug B). The low-adherence contrast remains somewhat amplified compared to modern INSTI-versus-comparator settings to preserve visible methodological signal; this stylization is intentional and is not a claim about any specific product pair. This DGP was not formally calibrated to empirical data from a specific trial or cohort.

Teaching code: oracle truth via Monte Carlo 
# ── Oracle counterfactual risks ───────────────────────────────────────────
# Because we control the DGP, we can compute the TRUE risk under each
# (drug, adherence) regime by simulating a large population (n = 100,000)
# with adherence forced to a constant level at every time point.
# This serves as the ground truth against which estimators are evaluated.

library(simhelpers)  # or source("R/sim_oral_forgiveness_helpers.R")

oracle_regimes <- list(
  DrugA_z1    = list(a0 = 1, z_level = 1L),    # Drug A, low adherence
  DrugA_z2    = list(a0 = 1, z_level = 2L),    # Drug A, medium adherence
  DrugA_z3    = list(a0 = 1, z_level = 3L),    # Drug A, high adherence
  DrugB_z1    = list(a0 = 0, z_level = 1L),    # Drug B, low adherence
  DrugB_z2    = list(a0 = 0, z_level = 2L),    # Drug B, medium adherence
  DrugB_z3    = list(a0 = 0, z_level = 3L),    # Drug B, high adherence
  DrugA_shift = list(a0 = 1, z_level = "shift"), # Drug A, shift policy
  DrugB_shift = list(a0 = 0, z_level = "shift")  # Drug B, shift policy
)

oracle_truth <- oracle_oral_forgiveness(
  regimes  = oracle_regimes,
  K        = 4,             # number of time blocks
  n_oracle = 100000,        # large MC sample for precise estimates
  seed     = 99999,
  params   = dgp_params     # same DGP parameters as the tutorial
)
# Returns data.frame with columns: regime, a0, z_target, cum_risk_oracle
print(oracle_truth)

The simulated cohort of 20,000 patients is randomised 1:1 to Drug A (more forgiving) or Drug B (less forgiving), followed over \(K = 4\) blocks of 90 days (~1 year). Baseline covariates include age (standardized, mean 0 SD 1), disease severity, depression, and prior treatment instability. At each block, time-varying health status \(L_t\) (continuous) depends on prior adherence \(Z_{t-1}\) (with AR(1) persistence), and categorical adherence \(Z_t \in \{1, 2, 3\}\) (low, medium, high) depends strongly on current health \(L_t\) (\(\beta_{Z \leftarrow L} = 0.80\)), creating treatment-confounder feedback that biases naive analyses. The key feature of this DGP that makes naive bias difficult to remove is that \(L_t\) is continuous and strongly predicts failure (\(\beta_{Y \leftarrow L} = -0.80\)): conditioning on the coarser \(Z_t\) alone leaves substantial residual variation in \(L_t\) within each adherence stratum. Adherence history is captured implicitly through the \(L_t\) AR(1) process; the explicit \(\bar{Z}\) term is set to zero (\(\beta_{Y \leftarrow \bar{Z}} = 0\)), meaning history flows through \(L_t\) rather than a separate running mean. LTMLE accounts for this by sequentially adjusting for \(L_t\) at each time point, propagating the full clinical trajectory through the g-computation.

The forgiveness mechanism is encoded as a drug-by-adherence interaction in the failure hazard: Drug A has smaller risk penalties at low and medium adherence than Drug B. Oracle counterfactual risks (computed from 1e+05 MC draws) confirm that both drugs perform similarly at high adherence but diverge sharply as adherence declines, with Drug B’s risk rising much more steeply. Full DGP parameters (Table 21), baseline characteristics (Table 22), adherence distributions (Figure 15), cumulative incidence (Figure 16), and oracle counterfactual risks (Table 23) are in Section 13.3.

Before estimating risks under forced adherence regimes, the three identification assumptions formalised in Section 13.2 must hold, but the one most tied to the observed data is practical positivity. Every adherence level must have positive probability given the measured covariates at each time point. For static adherence interventions, positivity becomes increasingly challenging as the number of time points \(K\) grows. Even if the marginal probability of adhering at level \(z\) at any single block is reasonable (say 25%), the probability of maintaining that level across all \(K = 4\) blocks decays as roughly \(0.25^4 \approx 0.4\%\). This multiplicative erosion of support is the fundamental reason that static adherence regimes become fragile over extended follow-up, and it motivates the complementary LMTP shift-policy approach in Section 6. For a full discussion including adherence path-support tables, the consequences of weak support for estimator stability, practical guidance on when to shift from static regimes to feasible policies, and quantitative ESS and weight diagnostics, see Section 13.4 and Section 13.6.

5 Longitudinal TMLE Analysis of treatment contrasts under static adherence

We now estimate the primary forgiveness-curve estimand, the regimen-specific counterfactual risk under static adherence regimes, using longitudinal targeted minimum loss-based estimation (LTMLE). The idea is straightforward: fit a sequence of outcome models that predict virologic failure at each time block given the patient’s prior adherence and clinical history, then adjust those predictions using estimated adherence probabilities (the “targeting” step) to reduce bias and align inference with the causal estimand. The result is doubly robust: the estimate is consistent if either the outcome model or the adherence model is correctly specified (Laan and Gruber 2012; Lendle et al. 2017).

5.1 Encoding adherence for ltmle

The ltmle R package (Lendle et al. 2017) requires binary treatment nodes. We encode categorical adherence (\(Z_t \in \{1,2,3\}\)) as two binary dummies per block:

  • \(Z_{\text{med},t} = \mathbb{1}(Z_t \geq 2)\), medium adherence
  • \(Z_{\text{high},t} = \mathbb{1}(Z_t \geq 3)\), high adherence

The combination \((Z_{\text{med},t} = 0, Z_{\text{high},t} = 1)\) means high adherence; \((1, 0)\) means medium; \((0, 0)\) means low. The combination \((1, 1)\) is structurally impossible and is enforced by a deterministic g-function (det_g_oral() in sim_oral_forgiveness_helpers.R), which sets \(P(Z_{\text{high},t} = 1 \mid Z_{\text{med},t} = 0) = 0\) at each time point.

In addition, drug assignment \(A_0\) is included as a treatment node \(A_{\text{trt},t}\) at each block (constant across time, equal to \(A_0\)). This creates \(3 \times K = 12\) treatment nodes total, with a deterministic constraint that \(A_{\text{trt},t} = A_{\text{trt},1}\) for \(t > 1\).

5.1.1 Node ordering per block

The column ordering in the ltmle data frame determines the causal ordering:

\[L_t \to A_{\text{trt},t} \to Z_{\text{med},t} \to Z_{\text{high},t} \to Y_t\]

Health status \(L_t\) precedes all treatment nodes within each block, as required by the causal structure. Drug assignment \(A_{\text{trt},t}\) (constant, deterministic given \(A_{\text{trt},1}\)) precedes the adherence dummies.

SuperLearner library. All LTMLE and LMTP estimations in this tutorial use SL.glm (logistic regression) as the sole SuperLearner candidate. This is a deliberate choice for reproducibility and speed in a teaching context: because the DGP is parametric and the outcome model is correctly specified by a GLM, there is no bias from restricting the library. In applied work, a richer ensemble, e.g., c("SL.mean","SL.glm","SL.glmnet","SL.gam", "SL.ranger", "SL.earth") — is strongly recommended to guard against model misspecification. The teaching code blocks below show a richer library for reference.

5.2 LTMLE results

Show code 
if (!is.null(ltmle_results) && !is.null(oracle_truth)) {
  lr <- ltmle_results
  lr$arm <- ifelse(lr$a0 == 1, "Drug A", "Drug B")
  lr$adherence <- c("Low", "Medium", "High")[lr$z_level]
  lr$oracle <- sapply(seq_len(nrow(lr)), function(i) {
    ot_row <- oracle_truth[oracle_truth$a0 == lr$a0[i] &
                            oracle_truth$z_target == as.character(lr$z_level[i]), ]
    if (nrow(ot_row) > 0) ot_row$cum_risk_oracle[1] else NA_real_
  })

  print(kable(lr[, c("arm", "adherence", "estimate", "se", "CI_lower", "CI_upper", "oracle")],
        digits = 4,
        col.names = c("Arm", "Adherence", "LTMLE Risk", "SE", "95% CI Lower",
                       "95% CI Upper", "Oracle"),
        align = "llrrrrr") |>
    kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "condensed"), full_width = FALSE))
} else {
  no_results_msg("LTMLE regime estimates")
}
LTMLE implementation code 
library(ltmle)
source("R/sim_oral_forgiveness_helpers.R")

# Step 1: Simulate data
dat <- simulate_oral_forgiveness(n = 20000, K = 4, seed = 12345)

# Step 2: Prepare data for ltmle (joint drug + adherence treatment nodes)
# This creates a wide data frame with columns ordered as:
#   W_age, W_sev, W_dep, W_instab,
#   L_1, A_trt_1, Z_med_1, Z_high_1, Y_1,
#   L_2, A_trt_2, Z_med_2, Z_high_2, Y_2, ...
prep <- prep_ltmle_oral(dat, K = 4)

# Step 3: Define abar vectors for BOTH regimes at a given adherence level
# We pass abar as a list to get the within-call Drug A vs Drug B contrast
# with IC-based standard errors for the risk difference and risk ratio.
abar_drugA_high <- make_abar_oral(a0 = 1, z_level = 3, K = 4)
abar_drugB_high <- make_abar_oral(a0 = 0, z_level = 3, K = 4)

# Step 4: Fit ltmle with BOTH regimes in one call
# Using abar = list(treatment, control) gives IC-based RD and RR
# The deterministic g-function encodes two constraints:
#   1. A_trt_t = A_trt_1 for t > 1 (drug doesn't change)
#   2. Z_high_t = 0 whenever Z_med_t = 0 (structural impossibility)
fit <- ltmle::ltmle(
  data   = prep$dat_wide,
  Anodes = prep$Anodes,   # A_trt_1, Z_med_1, Z_high_1, ..., A_trt_K, Z_med_K, Z_high_K
  Lnodes = prep$Lnodes,   # W_age, W_sev, W_dep, W_instab, L_1, ..., L_K
  Ynodes = prep$Ynodes,   # Y_1, ..., Y_K
  survivalOutcome = TRUE,  # Y is absorbing; returns RISK (not survival)
  abar   = list(treatment = abar_drugA_high, control = abar_drugB_high),
  SL.library = "SL.glm",
  gbounds = c(0.005, 1),
  estimate.time = FALSE,
  deterministic.g.function = det_g_oral
)

# Step 5: Extract contrast estimates from summary
s  <- summary(fit)
em <- s$effect.measures

# Individual regime risks
risk_A <- em$treatment$estimate  # P(failure | Drug A, high adherence)
risk_B <- em$control$estimate    # P(failure | Drug B, high adherence)

# IC-based risk difference (treatment - control) and risk ratio
RD    <- em$ATE$estimate        # Drug A risk - Drug B risk
se_RD <- em$ATE$std.dev
RR    <- em$RR$estimate         # Drug A risk / Drug B risk

# Step 6: Run all 3 contrasts (one per adherence level)
# estimate_ltmle() makes 3 ltmle calls, each contrasting Drug A vs B
all_results <- estimate_ltmle(dat, K = 4, sl_lib = "SL.glm")
# Returns: list($risks = regime-specific estimates, $contrasts = RD/RR per z)

5.3 LTMLE treatment contrasts

Each LTMLE call estimates the Drug A risk and Drug B risk simultaneously at a given adherence level, then computes the risk difference (RD = Drug A risk − Drug B risk) and risk ratio (RR = Drug A risk / Drug B risk) with influence-curve-based standard errors. This within-call approach produces properly calibrated inference for the contrast, avoiding the instability that can arise from dividing two separately estimated quantities.

At low adherence, \(\text{RR} < 1\): Drug A has lower risk than Drug B (the forgiveness advantage). At high adherence, the ratio is closer to 1, confirming that the drugs perform similarly when patients are fully adherent. Note that risk ratios become less stable than risk differences when one or both risks approach the boundary, especially near zero, because division amplifies estimation noise in sparse-support strata. For this reason, we treat the risk difference as the primary contrast measure throughout, with the risk ratio serving as a secondary descriptive summary.

A note on finite-sample variability

Some regime-specific LTMLE estimates in Table 3 have wide confidence intervals or point estimates that deviate from the oracle truth. This reflects finite-sample variability rather than a flaw in the estimator.

Constant-adherence regimes (e.g., all-low or all-high across four blocks) have weak support: few patients naturally follow the same adherence level at every visit. As a result, the estimated treatment weights can become extreme, increasing reliance on the outcome model and leading to higher variance. With \(K = 4\) and 12 treatment nodes, regime-specific estimates can be unstable in a single estimate.

The LTMLE estimates in these tables come from one illustrative sample; visible discrepancies from oracle truth in a single realization do not contradict good average Monte Carlo performance (Section 9.7), and are especially expected for weakly supported static adherence paths.

The Monte Carlo study in Section 9.7 illustrates that LTMLE substantially reduces confounding bias at medium and high adherence relative to naive estimation on average across repeated samples, even when individual estimates appear noisy. At low adherence (\(z = 1\)), positivity constraints limit this advantage: sparse support for the forced-low-adherence path causes LTMLE’s absolute bias to exceed naive’s, though LTMLE correctly inflates its standard errors and maintains near-nominal coverage. Implementation details for the LTMLE analysis are provided in the code block below (click to expand), and the appendix section on positivity shows how regime support can be evaluated in the data.

Static adherence regimes, in which adherence is fixed at a specified level over time, define a clear causal estimand but may not correspond to feasible real-world interventions. In practice, adherence cannot be deterministically set to a fixed level for all individuals without specifying the underlying mechanism by which that level is achieved. Modified treatment policies (LMTPs) address this limitation by defining interventions that shift adherence relative to its observed value, thereby remaining within the support of the data. Importantly, this distinction is not only computational but conceptual: static regimes and LMTPs generally target different causal estimands. Static regimes represent idealized intervention targets, whereas LMTPs represent feasible policy-relevant interventions that can be more reliably estimated from observational data.

6 Modified treatment policies via LMTP

The LTMLE analysis above estimated the forgiveness curve under static adherence regimes. This section introduces an alternative approach using the lmtp package based on longitudinal modified treatment policies (LMTP). The LMTP framework can recover the same static estimand as a robustness check, but its primary advantage is that it enables estimation of more realistic causal effects that are less sensitive to positivity violations.

Rather than asking what would happen if everyone followed the same fixed adherence level, LMTP asks what would happen under realistic shifts from each person’s observed adherence trajectory. A useful way to interpret modified treatment policies is through concrete adherence shifts. For example, rather than defining an intervention that sets all individuals to a fixed adherence category of “high” — which may be unrealistic and unsupported by the data — an LMTP might instead shift each individual’s adherence upward by one category where possible: those already at high adherence remain unchanged, those at medium adherence shift to high, and those at low adherence shift to medium. Such interventions preserve the relative ordering of adherence observed in the data rather than replacing each individual’s adherence trajectory entirely. By operating within the observed support of adherence behavior, LMTPs avoid the extreme extrapolation required by static regimes and yield more stable and interpretable estimates.

More generally, three classes of interventions can be considered in this setting:

  1. Dynamic regimes assign treatment deterministically based on patient history (e.g., “patients with PDC < 80% receive adherence support”).
  2. Stochastic regimes assign treatment probabilistically (e.g., “patients receive adherence support with 50% probability at each visit”).
  3. Modified treatment policies (MTPs) modify each patient’s observed treatment (e.g., “increase each patient’s adherence if feasible”).

Dynamic and stochastic regimes fully replace the observed treatment process. In contrast, modified treatment policies perturb the treatment that actually occurred. In the adherence setting, this distinction has practical implications. Because adherence cannot be directly assigned, dynamic and stochastic regimes implicitly assume control over patient behavior at each time point. This creates interpretability challenges and can exacerbate positivity issues, as the resulting treatment patterns are often sparsely represented in the data. Modified treatment policies avoid this issue by defining interventions locally around the observed data. Rather than specifying an entirely new treatment process, they ask how outcomes would change under incremental improvements to what patients actually did.

6.1 From static regimes to modified treatment policies

The static-intervention estimand \(\psi(a_0, z)\) defines the forgiveness curve, but it corresponds to an intervention with no clear real-world analogue: setting every patient’s adherence to exactly level \(z\) at every time point. In practice, adherence is shaped by tolerability, patient circumstances, and clinical status, and cannot be deterministically maintained over time.

This leads to two limitations. First, limited support: only a small fraction of patients follow any fixed adherence trajectory across all \(K\) blocks, so estimation under static regimes relies on extrapolation beyond the observed data. Second, limited policy relevance: clinical decisions typically aim to improve adherence (e.g., counseling or reminders), rather than enforce a fixed level for all patients.

Modified treatment policies address both issues by defining interventions relative to each patient’s observed adherence (Díaz, Williams, et al. 2021, 2023). The shift policy used in this tutorial is:

\[ d(Z_t) = \min(Z_t + 1, 3) \]

This intervention increases adherence by one category (low \(\to\) medium, medium \(\to\) high, high \(\to\) high), with a cap at the maximum. It represents a plausible intervention, such as the average effect of an adherence support program. Because the intervention makes only local changes to observed adherence, it requires substantially weaker support in the data.

The corresponding estimand is:

\[ \psi^d = E\!\left[Y_K^{d(\bar{Z})}\right] \]

This parameter asks: “what would the risk of virologic failure be if each patient’s adherence were incrementally improved at each visit?” It targets a feasible intervention and yields stable estimates even when constant adherence trajectories are rare.

6.2 LMTP results

We include A0 (drug assignment) as the first treatment node alongside Z_0, Z_1, \ldots, Z_K, fitting on the full dataset. The shift function forces A0 to the target drug value while shifting each Z_t up by one category.

Show code 
lmtp_out <- load_cached("lmtp_v13", quote(
  suppressMessages(suppressWarnings(
    estimate_lmtp(dat, K = K, folds = 1L, sl_lib = "SL.glm")
  ))
))
# estimate_lmtp() returns list($risks, $contrast)
lmtp_results <- if (!is.null(lmtp_out)) lmtp_out$risks else NULL
lmtp_shift_contrast <- if (!is.null(lmtp_out)) lmtp_out$contrast else NULL
Show code 
if (!is.null(lmtp_results) && !is.null(oracle_truth)) {
  lr <- lmtp_results
  lr$arm <- ifelse(lr$a0 == 1, "Drug A", "Drug B")
  lr$oracle <- sapply(lr$a0, function(a) {
    oracle_truth$cum_risk_oracle[oracle_truth$a0 == a &
                                  oracle_truth$z_target == "shift"]
  })
  print(kable(lr[, c("arm", "estimate", "se", "CI_lower", "CI_upper", "oracle")],
        digits = 4,
        col.names = c("Arm", "LMTP Risk", "SE", "95% CI Lower",
                       "95% CI Upper", "Oracle"),
        align = "lrrrrr") |>
    kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "condensed"), full_width = FALSE))

  # Show the shift contrast (RD and RR)
  if (!is.null(lmtp_shift_contrast)) {
    cat("\n\n**Shift contrast (Drug A vs Drug B):**",
        sprintf("RD = %.4f (SE = %.4f), RR = %.4f",
                lmtp_shift_contrast$RD, lmtp_shift_contrast$se_RD,
                lmtp_shift_contrast$RR), "\n")
  }
} else {
  no_results_msg("LMTP shift estimates")
}

6.3 LMTP-static: density-ratio route to the static-regime estimand

LMTP can also target the same static-regime estimand as LTMLE by using a static shift function that forces each \(Z_t\) to a constant value rather than shifting it by one category. This provides a density-ratio-based route to the same causal contrast as LTMLE, using different working models (density ratios rather than sequential regression). If both estimators target the same parameter and produce similar answers, this strengthens confidence in the result; if they diverge, the discrepancy signals sensitivity to modelling assumptions or positivity issues. The LMTP-shift estimator, by contrast, targets a different and complementary feasible-policy estimand. LMTP does not merely provide a numerically more stable estimator of the same target; it changes the intervention and therefore targets a different causal quantity that remains closer to observed adherence behavior. Note that mtp = FALSE is used for LMTP-static because the intervention sets adherence to a fixed value rather than modifying the observed value.

Implementation details for the LMTP shift and static analyses are in the code blocks below (click to expand).

LMTP-shift implementation code 
library(lmtp)
source("R/sim_oral_forgiveness_helpers.R")

dat <- simulate_oral_forgiveness(n = 20000, K = 4, seed = 12345)

# ── Data preparation with A0 as first treatment node ──────────────────
# A0 (drug assignment) is included as the first treatment node alongside
# Z_0, Z_1, ..., Z_K. This enables lmtp_contrast() to produce proper
# IC-based inference for the Drug A vs Drug B contrast on the full data.
# Y_neg1 is a placeholder outcome (always 0) so trt and outcome lengths match.
prep <- prep_lmtp_oral(dat, K = 4)

# ── Define the joint shift function ──────────────────────────────────
# lmtp requires: function(data, trt) -> named list of shifted values
# For Drug A (a0=1): force A0=1, shift Z_t to min(Z_t + 1, 3)
shift_fn_drugA <- make_joint_shift(a0_val = 1)
shift_fn_drugB <- make_joint_shift(a0_val = 0)
# The shift function handles each trt node:
#   A0:   forced to a0_val (drug assignment)
#   Z_0:  kept unchanged (placeholder)
#   Z_t:  shifted to min(Z_t + 1, 3)

# ── Fit LMTP for each drug on the FULL dataset ──────────────────────
fit_A <- lmtp::lmtp_tmle(
  data     = prep$data,
  trt      = prep$trt_nodes,        # c("A0", "Z_0", "Z_1", ..., "Z_4")
  outcome  = prep$outcome_nodes,    # c("Y_neg1", "Y_0", "Y_1", ..., "Y_4")
  baseline = prep$baseline,         # c("W_age", "W_sev", "W_dep", "W_instab")
  time_vary = prep$time_vary,       # list(NULL, NULL, "L_1", ..., "L_4")
  shift    = shift_fn_drugA,
  mtp      = TRUE,                  # TRUE for modified treatment policies
  outcome_type = "survival",
  folds    = 5L,
  learners_trt = "SL.glm",
  learners_outcome = "SL.glm"
)
fit_B <- lmtp::lmtp_tmle(
  data = prep$data, trt = prep$trt_nodes, outcome = prep$outcome_nodes,
  baseline = prep$baseline, time_vary = prep$time_vary,
  shift = shift_fn_drugB, mtp = TRUE, outcome_type = "survival",
  folds = 5L, learners_trt = "SL.glm", learners_outcome = "SL.glm"
)

# ── IC-based contrast: Drug A vs Drug B ──────────────────────────────
# lmtp_contrast() computes the difference on the SURVIVAL scale;
# for risk, negate the additive contrast: RD(risk) = -(surv_A - surv_B)
rd_contrast <- lmtp::lmtp_contrast(fit_A, ref = fit_B, type = "additive")
rd_tidy <- generics::tidy(rd_contrast)
RD <- -rd_tidy$estimate          # risk difference (Drug A - Drug B)
se_RD <- rd_tidy$std.error       # IC-based SE

# ── Wrapper: run Drug A vs B shift contrast in one call ──────────────
all_lmtp <- estimate_lmtp(dat, K = 4, folds = 5L, sl_lib = "SL.glm")
# Returns: list($risks = per-arm risk estimates, $contrast = RD and RR)
LMTP-static implementation code 
library(lmtp)
source("R/sim_oral_forgiveness_helpers.R")

dat <- simulate_oral_forgiveness(n = 20000, K = 4, seed = 12345)

# ── Data preparation (same as for LMTP-shift) ────────────────────────
prep <- prep_lmtp_oral(dat, K = 4)

# ── Define a STATIC shift function ───────────────────────────────────
# Unlike the shift policy, the static function forces Z_t to a constant
# value (e.g., z = 3 for high adherence) at every time point.
# mtp = FALSE because this is a deterministic static intervention.
static_fn_drugA_high <- make_joint_static(a0_val = 1, z_val = 3)
static_fn_drugB_high <- make_joint_static(a0_val = 0, z_val = 3)
# The function handles each trt node:
#   A0:   forced to a0_val
#   Z_0:  kept unchanged (placeholder)
#   Z_t:  forced to z_val (e.g., 3 = high)

# ── Fit LMTP-static for each drug on the FULL dataset ────────────────
# Note: mtp = FALSE for static interventions
fit_A_static <- lmtp::lmtp_tmle(
  data     = prep$data,
  trt      = prep$trt_nodes,
  outcome  = prep$outcome_nodes,
  baseline = prep$baseline,
  time_vary = prep$time_vary,
  shift    = static_fn_drugA_high,
  mtp      = FALSE,                  # FALSE for static (deterministic) policy
  outcome_type = "survival",
  folds    = 5L,
  learners_trt = "SL.glm",
  learners_outcome = "SL.glm"
)
fit_B_static <- lmtp::lmtp_tmle(
  data = prep$data, trt = prep$trt_nodes, outcome = prep$outcome_nodes,
  baseline = prep$baseline, time_vary = prep$time_vary,
  shift = static_fn_drugB_high, mtp = FALSE, outcome_type = "survival",
  folds = 5L, learners_trt = "SL.glm", learners_outcome = "SL.glm"
)

# ── IC-based contrast ────────────────────────────────────────────────
rd_contrast <- lmtp::lmtp_contrast(fit_A_static, ref = fit_B_static,
                                    type = "additive")
rd_tidy <- generics::tidy(rd_contrast)
RD <- -rd_tidy$estimate          # risk difference (Drug A - Drug B)
se_RD <- rd_tidy$std.error

# ── Wrapper: run all 3 adherence levels in one call ──────────────────
all_static <- estimate_lmtp_static(dat, K = 4, folds = 5L, sl_lib = "SL.glm")
# Returns: list($risks = 6 regime-specific estimates,
#               $contrasts = 3 Drug A vs B contrasts at z = 1, 2, 3)

6.4 Secondary treatment-comparison estimand

As a secondary analysis complementing the primary forgiveness-curve estimation, regimens may also be compared under a shared adherence distribution. One implemented approach maps both treatment arms to the observed adherence distribution of one reference regimen using an LMTP shift: baseline treatment assignment is jointly intervened upon alongside first-period adherence, and subsequent adherence values are shifted block-wise based on the observed between-regimen adherence gap, with resulting values bounded within feasible support. Because adherence is treated as continuous and the intervention modifies the natural value of each patient’s adherence trajectory, LMTP is the appropriate estimator. This contrast addresses a controlled-adherence question rather than a natural-course effectiveness comparison: it isolates differences in regimen-specific outcome risk apart from adherence differences attributable to each regimen’s side-effect profile or other adherence pathways. The reverse standardization — both arms mapped to the reference treatment’s adherence — provides a complementary perspective on the same question. Natural-course regimen comparisons, in which adherence and monitoring evolve as observed under each regimen, are conceptually distinct and are noted as an extension in Section 13.10.

6.5 Continuous adherence via proportion of days covered

The analyses above treated adherence as a three-level categorical variable. In practice, pharmacy refill data and electronic monitoring often yield a continuous measure: the proportion of days covered (PDC), defined as the fraction of days in each monitoring block during which the patient had medication available. Continuous PDC preserves more information than a categorised version and enables richer interventions, such as “increase every patient’s adherence by 10 percentage points” rather than “shift everyone up one category.”

LMTP handles continuous treatments natively. The shift function \(d(Z_t) = \min(Z_t + \delta, 1)\) nudges each patient’s observed PDC upward by \(\delta\), capping at 1.0 (perfect adherence). Because this is a modified treatment policy, it respects the natural bounds: a patient already at 95% PDC is shifted only to 100%, not beyond, avoiding positivity violations that would arise from a static intervention like “set PDC = 1.0 for everyone.”

6.5.1 Preparing LMTP data with continuous PDC

The data preparation follows the same structure as the categorical LMTP analysis, but with continuous PDC_t replacing categorical Z_t as the treatment node. The drug assignment A0 remains the first treatment node so that contrasts between drugs use IC-based inference.

Prepare LMTP data with continuous PDC 
# ── Build wide dataset for lmtp with continuous PDC ───────────────────────
baseline_pdc <- c("W_age", "W_sev", "W_dep", "W_instab")

wide_pdc <- dat_pdc[, baseline_pdc, drop = FALSE]
wide_pdc$id <- seq_len(nrow(dat_pdc))
wide_pdc$A0 <- dat_pdc$A0
wide_pdc$PDC_0 <- round(runif(nrow(dat_pdc), 0.5, 0.9), 4)  # placeholder
wide_pdc$Y_neg1 <- 0L
wide_pdc$Y_0 <- 0L

trt_pdc     <- c("A0", "PDC_0")
outcome_pdc <- c("Y_neg1", "Y_0")
tv_pdc      <- list(NULL, NULL)

for (tt in seq_len(K)) {
  wide_pdc[[paste0("L_", tt)]]   <- dat_pdc[[paste0("L_", tt)]]
  wide_pdc[[paste0("PDC_", tt)]] <- dat_pdc[[paste0("PDC_", tt)]]
  wide_pdc[[paste0("Y_", tt)]]   <- dat_pdc[[paste0("Y_", tt)]]

  trt_pdc     <- c(trt_pdc, paste0("PDC_", tt))
  outcome_pdc <- c(outcome_pdc, paste0("Y_", tt))
  tv_pdc[[tt + 2]] <- paste0("L_", tt)
}

# LOCF and absorbing state
for (i in seq_len(nrow(wide_pdc))) {
  event_occurred <- FALSE
  last_L <- 0; last_PDC <- wide_pdc[i, "PDC_0"]
  for (tt in seq_len(K)) {
    ln <- paste0("L_", tt); pn <- paste0("PDC_", tt); yn <- paste0("Y_", tt)
    if (event_occurred) {
      wide_pdc[i, ln]  <- last_L
      wide_pdc[i, pn]  <- last_PDC
      wide_pdc[i, yn]  <- 1L
    } else {
      if (is.na(wide_pdc[i, ln]))  wide_pdc[i, ln]  <- last_L
      if (is.na(wide_pdc[i, pn]))  wide_pdc[i, pn]  <- last_PDC
      if (is.na(wide_pdc[i, yn]))  wide_pdc[i, yn]  <- 0L
      last_L <- wide_pdc[i, ln]; last_PDC <- wide_pdc[i, pn]
      if (wide_pdc[i, yn] == 1L) event_occurred <- TRUE
    }
  }
}

wide_pdc <- as.data.frame(wide_pdc)
# cat("Treatment nodes:", paste(trt_pdc, collapse = ", "), "\n")
# cat("Outcome nodes:", paste(outcome_pdc, collapse = ", "), "\n")
# cat("N =", nrow(wide_pdc), "| K =", K, "\n")

6.5.2 Analysis 1: Effect of a 10% PDC increase under Drug A

The first analysis asks: what would happen to virologic failure risk if every patient on Drug A had their adherence increased by 10 percentage points? This compares Drug A under observed adherence to Drug A under a feasible adherence-support intervention (e.g., text-message reminders, pharmacy counselling). The shift function \(d(\text{PDC}_t) = \min(\text{PDC}_t + 0.10, 1.0)\) ensures the intervention is bounded and feasible.

LMTP: Drug A observed vs Drug A +10% PDC shift 
# ── Shift function: Drug A observed adherence ─────────────────────────────
shift_A_observed <- function(data, trt) {
  out <- vector("list", length(trt))
  names(out) <- trt
  for (nm in trt) {
    if (nm == "A0") {
      out[[nm]] <- rep(1, nrow(data))    # force Drug A
    } else {
      out[[nm]] <- data[[nm]]            # observed PDC unchanged
    }
  }
  out
}

# ── Shift function: Drug A with +10% PDC ─────────────────────────────────
shift_A_plus10 <- function(data, trt) {
  out <- vector("list", length(trt))
  names(out) <- trt
  for (nm in trt) {
    if (nm == "A0") {
      out[[nm]] <- rep(1, nrow(data))    # force Drug A
    } else if (nm == "PDC_0") {
      out[[nm]] <- data[[nm]]            # placeholder unchanged
    } else {
      out[[nm]] <- pmin(data[[nm]] + 0.10, 1.0)  # shift +10%, cap at 1
    }
  }
  out
}

# ── Fit both models (cached) ──────────────────────────────────────────────
pdc_shift_res <- load_cached("pdc_shift_v13", quote({
  fit_A_obs_i <- lmtp::lmtp_tmle(
    data     = wide_pdc,
    trt      = trt_pdc,
    outcome  = outcome_pdc,
    baseline = baseline_pdc,
    time_vary = tv_pdc,
    shift    = shift_A_observed,
    mtp      = TRUE,
    outcome_type = "survival",
    folds    = 1L,
    learners_trt = "SL.glm",
    learners_outcome = "SL.glm"
  )
  fit_A_shift_i <- lmtp::lmtp_tmle(
    data     = wide_pdc,
    trt      = trt_pdc,
    outcome  = outcome_pdc,
    baseline = baseline_pdc,
    time_vary = tv_pdc,
    shift    = shift_A_plus10,
    mtp      = TRUE,
    outcome_type = "survival",
    folds    = 1L,
    learners_trt = "SL.glm",
    learners_outcome = "SL.glm"
  )
  ctr_i <- lmtp::lmtp_contrast(fit_A_shift_i, ref = fit_A_obs_i, type = "additive")
  ctr_df <- if (!is.null(ctr_i$estimates)) ctr_i$estimates else ctr_i$vals
  tidy_obs   <- generics::tidy(fit_A_obs_i)
  tidy_shift <- generics::tidy(fit_A_shift_i)
  list(
    tidy_A_obs   = tidy_obs,
    tidy_A_shift = tidy_shift,
    ctr_shift_df = ctr_df
  )
}))

# ── Extract cached results ───────────────────────────────────────────────
tidy_A_obs   <- pdc_shift_res$tidy_A_obs
tidy_A_shift <- pdc_shift_res$tidy_A_shift
ctr_shift_df <- pdc_shift_res$ctr_shift_df

# lmtp works on survival scale: estimate = P(survive)
# Risk = 1 - survival; RD(risk) = -(survival_shifted - survival_observed)
risk_A_obs   <- round(1 - tidy_A_obs$estimate, 4)
risk_A_shift <- round(1 - tidy_A_shift$estimate, 4)
RD_shift     <- round(-ctr_shift_df$estimate, 4)
se_shift     <- round(ctr_shift_df$std.error, 4)
ci_lo_shift  <- round(RD_shift - 1.96 * se_shift, 4)
ci_hi_shift  <- round(RD_shift + 1.96 * se_shift, 4)
Show code 
shift_df <- data.frame(
  Regime = c("Drug A, observed PDC", "Drug A, PDC + 10%", "Risk difference"),
  Risk = c(risk_A_obs, risk_A_shift, RD_shift),
  SE = c(round(tidy_A_obs$std.error, 4),
         round(tidy_A_shift$std.error, 4),
         se_shift),
  `95% CI` = c(
    paste0("[", round(1 - tidy_A_obs$conf.high, 4), ", ",
           round(1 - tidy_A_obs$conf.low, 4), "]"),
    paste0("[", round(1 - tidy_A_shift$conf.high, 4), ", ",
           round(1 - tidy_A_shift$conf.low, 4), "]"),
    paste0("[", ci_lo_shift, ", ", ci_hi_shift, "]")
  ),
  check.names = FALSE
)
kable(shift_df, align = "lrrr") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "condensed"), full_width = FALSE)

Table 5 shows the estimated effect of a realistic adherence-support intervention. The risk difference quantifies how much virologic failure would be reduced if an intervention (e.g., pharmacy counselling, text-message reminders) successfully increased every Drug A patient’s PDC by 10 percentage points. Because this is a modified treatment policy, the shift respects each patient’s natural adherence trajectory: patients already near 100% PDC receive a smaller effective boost, avoiding positivity violations.

6.5.3 Analysis 2: Drug A vs Drug B under Drug A’s observed adherence

The second analysis asks a different question: what if patients on Drug B had achieved the same adherence as patients on Drug A? This uses the observed PDC distribution of Drug A patients and asks what would have happened if Drug B patients followed that same distribution. This is relevant when the clinical question is whether the drug itself (rather than adherence behaviour) explains outcome differences.

To implement this, we use a shift function that forces Drug B while leaving PDC at its observed values. The contrast between the Drug A and Drug B fits — both under the Drug A population’s observed adherence — isolates the drug effect from the adherence effect.

LMTP: Drug A vs Drug B under Drug A’s observed adherence 
# ── Shift function: Drug B with observed PDC (unchanged) ──────────────────
shift_B_observed <- function(data, trt) {
  out <- vector("list", length(trt))
  names(out) <- trt
  for (nm in trt) {
    if (nm == "A0") {
      out[[nm]] <- rep(0, nrow(data))    # force Drug B
    } else {
      out[[nm]] <- data[[nm]]            # observed PDC unchanged
    }
  }
  out
}

# ── Fit Drug B models (cached) ────────────────────────────────────────────
shift_identity <- function(data, trt) {
  out <- vector("list", length(trt))
  names(out) <- trt
  for (nm in trt) out[[nm]] <- data[[nm]]
  out
}
idx_B <- which(wide_pdc$A0 == 0)

pdc_drug_res <- load_cached("pdc_drug_compare_v13", quote({
  # Drug B under observed adherence (full sample, force A0 = 0)
  fit_B_obs_i <- lmtp::lmtp_tmle(
    data     = wide_pdc,
    trt      = trt_pdc,
    outcome  = outcome_pdc,
    baseline = baseline_pdc,
    time_vary = tv_pdc,
    shift    = shift_B_observed,
    mtp      = TRUE,
    outcome_type = "survival",
    folds    = 1L,
    learners_trt = "SL.glm",
    learners_outcome = "SL.glm"
  )
  # Drug B under its OWN natural adherence (subset, identity shift)
  fit_B_natural_i <- lmtp::lmtp_tmle(
    data     = wide_pdc[idx_B, ],
    trt      = trt_pdc,
    outcome  = outcome_pdc,
    baseline = baseline_pdc,
    time_vary = tv_pdc,
    shift    = shift_identity,
    mtp      = TRUE,
    outcome_type = "survival",
    folds    = 1L,
    learners_trt = "SL.glm",
    learners_outcome = "SL.glm"
  )
  # Need fit_A_obs from pdc_shift_res for the contrast — refit if not cached
  fit_A_obs_i <- lmtp::lmtp_tmle(
    data     = wide_pdc,
    trt      = trt_pdc,
    outcome  = outcome_pdc,
    baseline = baseline_pdc,
    time_vary = tv_pdc,
    shift    = shift_A_observed,
    mtp      = TRUE,
    outcome_type = "survival",
    folds    = 1L,
    learners_trt = "SL.glm",
    learners_outcome = "SL.glm"
  )
  ctr_i <- lmtp::lmtp_contrast(fit_A_obs_i, ref = fit_B_obs_i, type = "additive")
  ctr_df <- if (!is.null(ctr_i$estimates)) ctr_i$estimates else ctr_i$vals
  list(
    tidy_B_obs = generics::tidy(fit_B_obs_i),
    tidy_B_nat = generics::tidy(fit_B_natural_i),
    ctr_drug_df = ctr_df
  )
}))

# ── Extract cached results ───────────────────────────────────────────────
tidy_B_obs <- pdc_drug_res$tidy_B_obs
tidy_B_nat <- pdc_drug_res$tidy_B_nat
ctr_drug_df <- pdc_drug_res$ctr_drug_df

risk_B_nat <- round(1 - tidy_B_nat$estimate, 4)
risk_B_obs <- round(1 - tidy_B_obs$estimate, 4)
RD_drug    <- round(-ctr_drug_df$estimate, 4)
se_drug    <- round(ctr_drug_df$std.error, 4)
ci_lo_drug <- round(RD_drug - 1.96 * se_drug, 4)
ci_hi_drug <- round(RD_drug + 1.96 * se_drug, 4)
Show code 
drug_df <- data.frame(
  Regime = c("Drug B, natural adherence",
             "Drug A, observed PDC",
             "Drug B, shifted to Drug A adherence",
             "Risk difference (A − B, same adherence)"),
  Risk = c(risk_B_nat, risk_A_obs, risk_B_obs, RD_drug),
  SE = c(round(tidy_B_nat$std.error, 4),
         round(tidy_A_obs$std.error, 4),
         round(tidy_B_obs$std.error, 4),
         se_drug),
  `95% CI` = c(
    paste0("[", round(1 - tidy_B_nat$conf.high, 4), ", ",
           round(1 - tidy_B_nat$conf.low, 4), "]"),
    paste0("[", round(1 - tidy_A_obs$conf.high, 4), ", ",
           round(1 - tidy_A_obs$conf.low, 4), "]"),
    paste0("[", round(1 - tidy_B_obs$conf.high, 4), ", ",
           round(1 - tidy_B_obs$conf.low, 4), "]"),
    paste0("[", ci_lo_drug, ", ", ci_hi_drug, "]")
  ),
  check.names = FALSE
)
kable(drug_df, align = "lrrr") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "condensed"), full_width = FALSE)

Table 6 shows three estimates for Drug B alongside Drug A. The first row gives Drug B’s risk under its own natural adherence pattern (no intervention on PDC). The third row shows Drug B’s risk when adherence is shifted to match the observed PDC distribution of the full sample — the same adherence pattern used for the Drug A estimate. Comparing the first and third rows reveals how much of Drug B’s risk is attributable to its adherence pattern versus the drug itself. The risk difference in the fourth row isolates the drug effect by holding adherence constant: a negative value (Drug A − Drug B < 0) indicates Drug A is protective even when adherence is equalised.
