This contrast avoids the positivity issues that plague static-regime comparisons at uniformly low or high adherence, because it is identified at the observed adherence distribution.

Using continuous PDC rather than categorical adherence has several practical advantages for forgiveness studies. First, the analyst can ask “what if adherence increased by 5%, 10%, or 20%?” and trace out a dose-response curve for the adherence intervention, rather than being limited to discrete jumps between categories. Second, there is a higher chance of sufficient positivity, as A 10% shift in continuous PDC is a small perturbation of the observed distribution, ensuring strong overlap between the observed and shifted treatment densities. Categorical shifts (e.g., from low to medium) can involve larger distributional jumps with weaker positivity. Third, it is a more policy-relevant contrast, as decision-makers often want to know the expected benefit of a realistic adherence improvement (e.g., from a pharmacy programme), which maps naturally to a continuous PDC shift rather than a categorical reassignment. Lastly, comparing drugs under the population’s natural adherence pattern (as in Table 6) answers a realistic question that still removed confounding from differential adherence patterns: given how patients actually take their medication, which drug produces better outcomes? The trade-off is that continuous treatments require density-ratio estimation (the LMTP machinery), which is more complex than the IPTW approach used for categorical treatments in LTMLE. The lmtp package handles this internally via highly adaptive lasso or other flexible learners for the density ratios.

7 Forgiveness Curves

The forgiveness curve describes the counterfactual risk of virologic failure as a function of adherence level for each regimen. Formally, it represents \(E[Y^{a_0, \bar{z}}]\), the expected outcome if adherence were sustained at level \(z\) across all time points under a given regimen. A flatter curve indicates greater forgiveness, meaning that outcome risk increases more slowly as adherence declines.

It is important to distinguish this causal curve from observed adherence–outcome associations commonly reported in the HIV literature. Observed summaries such as \(E[Y \mid Z = z, A_0 = a_0]\) reflect the risk seen among patients who happen to achieve a given adherence level, and therefore conflate the causal effect of adherence with selection effects. In particular, patients who maintain high adherence tend to be healthier, while those with low adherence are often sicker, leading to biased comparisons. The causal forgiveness curve instead isolates the effect of adherence by defining risk under intervention, accounting for time-dependent confounding.

The figure comparison below illustrate this distinction: naive adherence-stratified estimates are typically steeper than causal estimates, overstating the effect of adherence. This reflects treatment–confounder feedback, commonly described as the “healthy adherer” effect: patients who maintain high adherence tend to be healthier, while those with low adherence are often sicker, biasing observed risk differences away from the causal effect.

In contrast, LTMLE estimates, which adjust for time-varying confounding, recover the oracle counterfactual risks in the simulation. The practical implication is that naive adherence-stratified summaries—such as “patients above X% adherence had Y% failure”—systematically overstate the steepness of the forgiveness curve and can distort comparisons between regimens.

Teaching code: naive adherence-stratified analysis 
# ── Naive adherence-stratified cumulative risk ──────────────────────────
# For each (drug, adherence level), compute observed per-block hazards
# among patients at risk with that Z value, then chain into cumulative risk.
# This is the standard "patients with adherence X had Y% failure" analysis
# that does NOT adjust for time-varying confounding.

results <- data.frame()
for (a0 in 0:1) {
  for (z in 1:3) {
    arm_idx <- which(dat$A0 == a0)
    surv <- 1.0

    for (tt in seq_len(K)) {
      # Who is at risk (not yet failed) at this block?
      if (tt == 1) {
        at_risk <- arm_idx
      } else {
        y_prev <- dat[[paste0("Y_", tt - 1)]][arm_idx]
        at_risk <- arm_idx[!is.na(y_prev) & y_prev == 0]
      }
      # Among at-risk patients, subset to those with Z_t == z
      z_t <- dat[[paste0("Z_", tt)]][at_risk]
      y_t <- dat[[paste0("Y_", tt)]][at_risk]
      in_stratum <- which(!is.na(z_t) & z_t == z)

      haz_t <- if (length(in_stratum) > 10) {
        mean(y_t[in_stratum], na.rm = TRUE)
      } else 0
      surv <- surv * (1 - haz_t)
    }
    results <- rbind(results, data.frame(
      a0 = a0, z_level = z, cum_risk = 1 - surv,
      arm = ifelse(a0 == 1, "Drug A", "Drug B")
    ))
  }
}
# NOTE: These estimates are BIASED by treatment-confounder feedback.
# Sicker patients adhere less, inflating observed risk at low adherence
# and deflating it at high adherence → steeper curves than truth.

7.1 Marginal structural model summary

To complement the nonparametric forgiveness curve, we fit a marginal structural model (MSM) (here a logistic working model to the six LTMLE-estimated regime-specific risks. The MSM compresses the curve into four interpretable coefficients, the most important being the drug \(\times\) adherence interaction \(\beta_3\), which provides a single-number summary of differential forgiveness and a formal Wald test of \(H_0\!: \beta_3 = 0\) (i.e., “do the two drugs differ in forgiveness?”). For the full MSM specification, fitted coefficients (Table 26), and a guide to interpreting each parameter, see Section 13.7.

7.2 Connection to modern ART adherence questions

The framework developed in this tutorial is not specific to any particular drug class, but rather to a class of causal questions about how treatment effectiveness depends on imperfect adherence. These questions remain central in modern ART, particularly as treatment modalities diversify.

Two broad types of comparisons are currently of interest:

  1. Oral regimen comparisons within high-barrier therapies.
    Contemporary questions often focus on differences between regimens that are all highly effective under perfect adherence, such as two-drug versus three-drug INSTI-based therapies. Here, the primary question is not whether regimens work, but how their risk of failure changes as adherence declines. The methods in this tutorial can be used to estimate and compare forgiveness curves across such regimens, defined as the counterfactual risk under sustained adherence patterns.

  2. Long-acting injectables versus daily oral therapy.
    Long-acting regimens (e.g., injectable cabotegravir-based therapies) fundamentally change the adherence process. Instead of daily pill-taking, adherence is determined by timely clinic attendance and the pharmacokinetics of drug depots. This shifts the causal question from “what happens under missed doses?” to “what happens under delayed or missed injections?”

From a methodological perspective, both settings can be addressed within the same framework by redefining the time-varying exposure:

  • For oral regimens, adherence is typically operationalized as proportion of days covered (PDC) over fixed intervals.
  • For injectable regimens, adherence can be defined in terms of injection timing, delays, and resulting drug concentration trajectories.

The causal estimand does not depend on how adherence is measured, but on how interventions are defined. For example:

  • Static regimes can represent sustained adherence levels (e.g., always high PDC or always on-time injections).
  • Dynamic regimes can represent clinical decision rules (e.g., intensified follow-up after missed visits).
  • Modified treatment policies can represent feasible improvements (e.g., reducing delays in injection visits or increasing average adherence by a fixed amount).

In this way, the LMTP and LTMLE approaches provide a unified framework for comparing regimens that differ not only in pharmacology, but also in the structure of adherence itself. The main requirement is to align the data-generating process and intervention definition with the adherence mechanism relevant to the treatment modality under study. Section 13.9.1 provides a concrete illustration of how this mapping works for long-acting injectables, including an illustrative forgiveness curve and estimand mapping table, and Section 13.9 presents a toy data-generating process for the injectable setting.

Note that these primary analyses use a simplified DGP where every patient’s outcome is directly observed, no one is lost to follow-up, and no one switches regimens. Real HIV cohorts involve all three complications: 1) informative monitoring (viral load is tested only intermittently, and testing frequency depends on clinical suspicion), 2) informative censoring (loss to follow-up is more common among sicker patients), and 3) treatment switching (patients may change regimens after virologic failure or intolerable side effects). The LTMLE framework accommodates each by expanding the node structure and introducing new estimand distinctions.

For a full discussion with an expanded DGP, monitoring diagnostics (masked failure rates, monitoring frequency by adherence level), censoring adjustments via IPCW, and treatment switching handled as either treatment-policy (L-node) or while-on-treatment (C-node) strategies, see Section 13.10. For an LTMLE analysis that extends the primary drug-by-adherence framework to allow time-varying drug switching, see Section 13.10.1.

8 Interventional Mediation: Analysis Applied to Long-Acting Injectables

The forgiveness framework generalises naturally to delivery-system comparisons — for example, long-acting injectable versus daily oral ART. For oral regimens, execution is day-to-day pill-taking (PDC); for injectables, it is visit attendance and injection timeliness. Both map onto the same causal chain — regimen \(\to\) execution \(\to\) pharmacologic exposure \(\to\) virologic failure — but the pharmacokinetic link differs: oral formulations track exposure almost linearly with PDC, whereas depot injectables provide sustained absorption that buffers against missed or delayed visits.

8.1 The decomposition question

The motivating causal question is:

How much of the difference in virologic failure risk between injectable and oral ART is attributable to improved pharmacologic coverage (the execution/forgiveness advantage), versus residual differences that persist even under equalised execution?

This decomposes the total delivery-system effect into an execution-mediated component (operating through coverage \(E_t\)) and a residual component (direct pharmacologic or non-PK mechanisms). The delivery-system comparison requires an extended SCM with:

  • \(A_0 \in \{\text{oral}, \text{LA}\}\): Delivery-system assignment.
  • \(Z_t\): Arm-specific execution (PDC for oral, injection timeliness for LA).
  • \(E_t\): Pharmacologic coverage (continuous mediator). For oral, \(E_t \approx \text{PDC}_t\); for LA, \(E_t\) evolves via exponential decay with dose timing.
  • \(Y_t\): Virologic failure (absorbing).

The DAG now includes \(A_0 \to Z_t \to E_t \to Y_t\) with \(E_t\) mediating the execution-outcome relationship, and a direct path \(A_0 \to Y_t\) for non-PK effects.

Two complementary strategies target the decomposition above: 1) LMTP shift policies for feasible execution improvements: the modified treatment policy \(d(E_t) = \min(E_t + \delta, 1)\) perturbs each patient’s observed coverage by a clinically plausible increment, avoiding the positivity violations inherent to static regimes on continuous \(E_t\). 2) Interventional mediation decomposition via crumble (Hejazi, Rudolph, and Díaz 2023; Díaz, Hejazi, et al. 2021): formally partition the total \(A_0 \to Y\) effect into direct, confounder-mediated, and coverage-mediated pathways, providing the mechanism-level decomposition that shift policies alone cannot.

The delivery-system analysis uses an illustrative DGP informed by the qualitative shape and scale of published trial data from ATLAS, FLAIR, and ATLAS-2M (Swindells et al. 2020; Orkin et al. 2021; Overton et al. 2021) — it is not formally calibrated to any specific product or trial endpoint. The DGP simulates a 48-week cohort (\(K = 6\) eight-week blocks) comparing long-acting injectable ART (\(A_0 = 1\)) versus daily oral ART (\(A_0 = 0\)). In the LA arm, execution reflects injection timeliness (on-time probability \(\approx 0.94\)) and pharmacologic coverage \(E_t\) evolves via exponential decay from the depot. In the oral arm, execution is continuous PDC and coverage tracks it linearly. Note that this delivery-system DGP is distinct from the simpler toy injectable DGP described in Section 13.9, which illustrates delay-based adherence mechanics in isolation without the mediation decomposition.

Show code 
if (!is.null(dat_v5ds)) {
  E_means <- data.frame(
    block = rep(1:K_ds, 2),
    arm = rep(c("Oral", "LA Injectable"), each = K_ds),
    mean_E = c(
      sapply(1:K_ds, function(t) mean(dat_v5ds[dat_v5ds$A0 == 0, paste0("E_", t)], na.rm = TRUE)),
      sapply(1:K_ds, function(t) mean(dat_v5ds[dat_v5ds$A0 == 1, paste0("E_", t)], na.rm = TRUE))
    )
  )
  ggplot(E_means, aes(x = block, y = mean_E, color = arm, group = arm)) +
    geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
    geom_point(size = 3) +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:K_ds, labels = paste0("Wk ", 8 * (1:K_ds))) +
    labs(x = "Time", y = "Mean Pharmacologic Coverage (E)", color = "Arm") +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
    theme(legend.position = "bottom") +
    coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0.5, 1))
}

Because the DGP is known, oracle truth — the counterfactual risk under forced constant coverage, computed via Monte Carlo simulation (\(n = 50{,}000\)) — establishes the target parameter free of estimation noise.

The gap between the LA and oral curves at each coverage level reflects the direct regimen effect (built into the DGP). The steeper slope of the oral curve indicates lower pharmacologic forgiveness — small coverage drops produce larger risk increases. Because pharmacologic coverage is continuous and its distribution differs markedly between arms (mean \(E_t \approx 0.99\) for LA versus \(\approx 0.76\) for oral), static interventions that force \(E_t\) to a common value violate positivity whenever the target falls outside the observed support. Estimation in this setting is more naturally framed using either feasible-policy interventions that perturb observed coverage or decomposition-based approaches that separate execution-mediated from residual effects.

One intuitive approach is to equalize execution across arms — force \(E_t = 1\) in both — and attribute the residual risk difference to non-PK mechanisms. The oracle curves above confirm that such a residual exists: the LA and oral curves do not converge at \(E_t = 1\). However, estimation of this contrast from data is impractical because perfect oral execution (\(\text{PDC} = 1.0\)) isn’t well supported by the observed distribution. Moreover, the equalized-execution approach cannot distinguish among the multiple pathways through which \(A_0\) affects \(Y\). Instead, we use a mediation analyses.

8.2 Interventional mediation decomposition

The delivery-system comparison raises a mechanistic question that a standard forgiveness curve cannot answer. A forgiveness curve for the injectable arm estimates the risk under forced constant coverage — but it does not tell us why the injectable arm outperforms the oral arm. Is it because depot pharmacokinetics maintain therapeutic drug levels (a pure PK mechanism, operating through coverage \(E_t\))? Or is part of the advantage attributable to other pathways, such as differences in immune recovery trajectory (CD4 changes, \(L_t\)) or unmeasured non-adherence factors (\(A_0 \to Y\) direct)? The LMTP shift-policy approach can quantify the aggregate benefit of feasible coverage improvements, but it cannot isolate whether the delivery-system advantage operates through pharmacologic coverage versus other pathways. Interventional mediation is therefore introduced to formally decompose the total effect into mechanism-specific components.

Note also that the sign pattern of the pathway estimates reflects the specific DGP parameterization. The p2 pathway (\(A_0 \to L_t \to Y\), approximately \(+4\) pp positive) arises because the LA arm assignment (\(A_0 = 1\)) does not directly improve adherence in the delivery-system DGP (the drug-to-adherence pathway operates differently here than in the oral-ART DGP), while the CD4-to-failure coefficient means that improved health status in the LA arm slightly reduces failure through the L channel in a way that partially offsets coverage benefits. This is a DGP artifact and should not be interpreted as a clinical finding about injectable versus oral ART. The sign pattern reflects the simulated parameterization and should not be interpreted as an empirical finding about injectable versus oral ART in clinical practice.

Shift policies quantify feasible improvements but cannot distinguish the mechanisms through which \(A_0\) affects \(Y\). The interventional mediation framework (Hejazi, Rudolph, and Díaz 2023; Díaz, Hejazi, et al. 2021) provides this decomposition, partitioning the total effect into four pathways: (1) \(A_0 \to Y\) direct (non-PK benefits), (2) \(A_0 \to L_t \to Y\) (via CD4/symptoms), (3) \(A_0 \to L_t \to E_t \to Y\) (via confounders then coverage), and (4) \(A_0 \to E_t \to Y\) (via coverage — the forgiveness mechanism). If pathway 4 dominates, the LA advantage is primarily pharmacokinetic.

The crumble package implements this decomposition using nonparametric efficiency theory. We fit the recanting-twins decomposition to a cross-sectional summary of the longitudinal delivery-system data.

Teaching code: interventional mediation decomposition with crumble 
library(crumble)

# ── Why interventional (not natural) mediation? ──────────────────────────
# Natural direct/indirect effects require cross-world counterfactuals:
# "the mediator value that WOULD occur under A=a, plugged into the
# outcome model under A=a'." When a mediator-outcome confounder L_t is
# affected by treatment (as here: CD4 depends on drug assignment),
# these cross-world quantities are not identified. Interventional effects
# replace the cross-world mediator with a STOCHASTIC draw from the
# mediator distribution under a reference treatment, avoiding the
# cross-world assumption entirely [Díaz et al., 2021].

# ── Load pre-built mediation data ─────────────────────────────────────────
# Cross-sectional summary saved by the manuscript (output/v13_cache/crumble_mediation_data.rds):
#   W1..W4 = baseline covariates (age, female, comorbidity, CD4)
#   A      = delivery-system assignment (1=LA, 0=oral)
#   Z      = intermediate confounder affected by A (mean CD4 over follow-up)
#   M      = mediator: mean pharmacologic coverage (mean E_t over follow-up)
#   Y      = virologic failure by 48 weeks
# Load pre-built data; drop any rows with NA (early failures → NaN in Z/M)
med_data <- na.omit(readRDS(file.path(cache_dir, "crumble_mediation_data.rds")))

# ── Fit crumble ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# d0/d1 define the counterfactual treatment assignments (reference/active).
# For binary A ∈ {0,1}: d0 sets everyone to oral (A=0), d1 to LA (A=1).
# effect = "RT" requests the recanting-twins 4-pathway decomposition,
# which is identified even with treatment-affected mediator-outcome
# confounders (Z). Alternatives: "RI" (2-way interventional), "N"
# (natural), "O" (organic).
fit_med <- crumble(
  data      = med_data,
  trt       = "A",                         # exposure: delivery-system assignment
  outcome   = "Y",                         # outcome: virologic failure
  mediators = "M",                         # mediator: pharmacologic coverage
  moc       = "Z",                         # mediator-outcome confounder (CD4)
  covar     = c("W1", "W2", "W3", "W4"),  # baseline covariates
  d0        = \(data, trt) rep(0, nrow(data)),  # reference: oral (A=0)
  d1        = \(data, trt) rep(1, nrow(data)),  # active: LA injectable (A=1)
  effect    = "RT",                        # recanting-twins 4-way decomposition
  learners  = "glm",                       # or c("glm", "ranger") for SL
  control   = crumble_control(
    crossfit_folds        = 2L,            # reduce from default 10 for speed
    mlr3superlearner_folds = 2L            # reduce from default 10 for speed
  )
)

results <- tidy(fit_med)
# The recanting-twins decomposition yields:
#   p1:  A → Y direct          (non-PK benefits)
#   p2:  A → Z → Y             (via CD4 changes, not through M)
#   p3:  A → Z → M → Y         (via CD4 then coverage)
#   p4:  A → M → Y             (via coverage: THE forgiveness mechanism)
#   direct  = p1 + p2           (all non-M pathways)
#   indirect = p3 + p4          (all M-mediated pathways)
#   ate     = direct + indirect (total effect)
# If p4 dominates → the LA advantage is primarily pharmacokinetic.
print(results)
# A tibble: 8 × 5
  estimand                  estimate std.error  conf.low conf.high
  <chr>                        <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>
1 p1                        0.00958   0.00101   0.00760    0.0116 
2 p2                        0.0268    0.00178   0.0233     0.0303 
3 p3                        0.000877  0.000168  0.000548   0.00121
4 p4                       -0.0433    0.000769 -0.0448    -0.0418 
5 intermediate_confounding -0.0148    0.00200  -0.0187    -0.0109 
6 ate                      -0.0209    0.00174  -0.0243    -0.0175 
7 indirect                 -0.0424    0.000775 -0.0439    -0.0409 
8 direct                    0.0364    0.00153   0.0334     0.0394
estimand estimate std.error conf.low conf.high
p1 0.0084 0.0006 0.0072 0.0097
p2 0.0359 0.0012 0.0334 0.0383
p3 0.0012 0.0001 0.0009 0.0014
p4 -0.0484 0.0006 -0.0495 -0.0472
intermediate_confounding -0.0239 0.0012 -0.0262 -0.0216
ate -0.0268 0.0010 -0.0287 -0.0250
indirect -0.0472 0.0006 -0.0483 -0.0461
direct 0.0443 0.0011 0.0421 0.0464

The recanting-twins decomposition reveals that the modest total effect of LA injectable versus oral ART (ATE ≈ −2.6 percentage points) masks two opposing forces. The coverage-mediated indirect effect (p3 + p4 ≈ −4.7 pp) confirms that LA’s advantage operates almost entirely through sustained pharmacologic coverage — the forgiveness mechanism. Pathway p4 alone (A → M → Y, −4.8 pp) captures the direct pharmacokinetic benefit: depot release maintains therapeutic drug levels even when patients miss clinic visits. However, non-coverage pathways (p1 + p2 ≈ +5.1 pp) partially offset this benefit, with the largest counter-current flowing through CD4 changes that affect failure through channels other than coverage (p2 ≈ +4.2 pp). The intermediate-confounding term (−2.9 pp) quantifies the bias that would arise from a naïve natural direct/indirect decomposition — precisely the identification problem that motivates the interventional approach.

For background on the injectable adherence construct and illustrative forgiveness curves, see Section 13.9.1; for details of the delivery-system DGP, see Section 13.9.

The analyses above illustrate the framework using a single simulated cohort. To assess whether the estimators recover the true causal estimand on average — and to characterise their bias, variance, and coverage properties — we turn to a repeated Monte Carlo study.

9 Monte Carlo Validation of Estimator Performance

The preceding sections demonstrate how an analyst would implement the forgiveness-curve analysis on a single dataset: defining the causal estimand, checking positivity, running LTMLE and LMTP, and interpreting the results. This section shifts perspective from tutorial demonstration to methodological validation. Using the same data-generating process with known ground truth, we evaluate the finite-sample properties of each estimator across 500 independent replications (\(n = 5,000\) per replication). The goal is to verify that the targeted estimators \(\Psi(\hat{P}_n)\) recover the true statistical parameter \(\Psi(P_0)\) with low bias, well-calibrated standard errors, and nominal confidence interval coverage, and to quantify where and why they struggle.

9.1 Simulation design

The Monte Carlo study uses the same DGP as the tutorial worked example (\(K = 4\) time blocks, parameters as defined in Section 4.1), with 500 independent replications and a sample size of \(n = 5,000\) per replication. The oracle truth is computed from \(N = 100{,}000\) draws from the same DGP, providing essentially exact reference risks against which estimators are evaluated.

9.2 Estimands and estimators evaluated

The estimators compared are: (1) adherence-stratified regression (naive, no causal adjustment); (2) LTMLE for static adherence regimes \(\psi(a_0, z)\) (primary causal estimand); (3) LMTP-static (a density-ratio-based route to the same static-regime causal contrast, included to compare estimation strategies for the same target parameter); and (4) LMTP-shift targeting \(\psi^d\) (a feasible shift policy \(d(Z_t) = \min(Z_t + 1, 3)\), a different but complementary estimand). Each estimator is evaluated against its own oracle truth, so bias and coverage numbers are valid within each row even though the shift parameter targets a different estimand. All nuisance models use SL.glm (logistic regression).

9.3 Evaluation metrics

  • Bias: Mean estimated value minus oracle truth.
  • SD: Empirical standard deviation across replications.
  • RMSE: Root mean squared error.
  • SE/SD: Ratio of mean estimated standard error to empirical SD (should be \(\approx 1.0\) for well-calibrated variance).
  • Coverage: Empirical coverage of the nominal 95% CI (should be \(\approx 0.95\)).
  • |Bias|/SD: Values \(< 0.5\) suggest bias is dominated by sampling noise.
  • Power: Proportion of replications with \(p < 0.05\) (rejection rate; interpretable as power when the true effect \(\neq 0\)).

9.4 Results: risk difference contrasts (primary)

Table 7 shows the RD contrasts, the primary, stable summary measure.

Show code 
if (!is.null(mc_summary)) {
  rd_rows <- mc_summary[grepl(" RD", mc_summary$estimand), ]
  if (nrow(rd_rows) > 0) {
    display_cols <- intersect(
      c("estimand", "truth", "mean_est", "bias", "sd", "rmse",
        "mean_se", "se_ratio", "coverage_95", "bias_var_ratio",
        "power_05", "n_valid"),
      names(rd_rows))
    print(kable(rd_rows[, display_cols], digits = 4,
                align = "lrrrrrrrrrrr", row.names = FALSE,
                col.names = c("Estimand", "Truth", "Mean Est", "Bias",
                              "SD", "RMSE", "Mean SE", "SE/SD",
                              "Coverage", "|Bias|/SD", "Power",
                              "n")[seq_along(display_cols)]) |>
      kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "condensed"),
                    full_width = FALSE, font_size = 9))
  }
} else {
  no_results_msg("Monte Carlo summary")
}

LMTP-static variance collapse: a substantive finding. The Monte Carlo results reveal a critical inferential failure for LMTP-static that goes beyond a minor calibration issue — not merely inflated bias but a complete breakdown of variance estimation at medium and high adherence. The mechanism and practical implications are detailed in the warning below.

LMTP-static inference at medium and high adherence is unreliable

The Monte Carlo results reveal a critical inferential failure for LMTP-static at medium and high adherence. At \(z = 2\), the SE/SD ratio is approximately 0.071, indicating that estimated standard errors are far too small; empirical coverage of the nominal 95% CI is only approximately 11%. At \(z = 3\), SE/SD \(\approx\) 0.97 with 89% coverage. This is a density-ratio collapse phenomenon: when the static-regime adherence path has very low probability under the observed data, the importance weights become extreme and the variance estimator breaks down. LMTP-static should not be used for inference at medium or high adherence in this setting. By contrast, LTMLE’s SE/SD ratios at these levels reflect approximately honest or mildly conservative variance estimation.

9.5 Results: risk ratio contrasts (secondary)

Table 8 shows RR contrasts. Note the extreme instability at \(z = 2, 3\) where both risks approach the boundary near zero; when one or both risks approach the boundary, division amplifies estimation noise in sparse-support strata, producing explosive replicates that corrupt the mean. Medians are used as location summaries throughout. The RR should be interpreted with caution and is provided as a secondary descriptive summary only.

Show code 
if (!is.null(mc_summary)) {
  rr_rows <- mc_summary[grepl(" RR", mc_summary$estimand), ]
  if (nrow(rr_rows) > 0) {
    display_cols <- intersect(
      c("estimand", "truth", "median_est", "bias_median", "sd", "rmse",
        "mean_se", "se_ratio", "coverage_95", "bias_var_ratio",
        "power_05", "n_valid"),
      names(rr_rows))
    print(kable(rr_rows[, display_cols], digits = 4,
                align = "lrrrrrrrrrrr", row.names = FALSE,
                col.names = c("Estimand", "Truth", "Median Est", "Med Bias",
                              "SD", "RMSE", "Mean SE", "SE/SD",
                              "Coverage", "|Bias|/SD", "Power",
                              "n")[seq_along(display_cols)]) |>
      kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "condensed"),
                    full_width = FALSE, font_size = 9))
  }
} else {
  no_results_msg("Monte Carlo summary")
}

9.6 Bias visualization

Figure 5 compares RD bias across estimators, the primary contrast measure. Figure 6 shows RR bias using the median rather than the mean (which is corrupted by extreme outliers at z = 2, 3).

Show code 
if (!is.null(mc_summary)) {
  rd_rows <- mc_summary[grepl(" RD", mc_summary$estimand), ]
  if (nrow(rd_rows) > 0 && any(!is.na(rd_rows$bias))) {
    rd_rows$estimator <- ifelse(grepl("Naive", rd_rows$estimand), "Naive",
                          ifelse(grepl("LTMLE", rd_rows$estimand), "LTMLE",
                          ifelse(grepl("LMTP-static", rd_rows$estimand), "LMTP-static",
                                 "LMTP-shift")))
    rd_rows$adherence <- ifelse(grepl("low", rd_rows$estimand), "low",
                          ifelse(grepl("medium", rd_rows$estimand), "medium",
                          ifelse(grepl("high", rd_rows$estimand), "high",
                          ifelse(grepl("z=1", rd_rows$estimand), "low",
                          ifelse(grepl("z=2", rd_rows$estimand), "medium",
                          ifelse(grepl("z=3", rd_rows$estimand), "high", "shift"))))))
    rd_rows$adherence <- factor(rd_rows$adherence,
                                 levels = c("low", "medium", "high", "shift"))

    ggplot(rd_rows[!is.na(rd_rows$bias), ],
           aes(x = adherence, y = bias, fill = estimator)) +
      geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.7), width = 0.6, alpha = 0.85) +
      geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40") +
      scale_fill_manual(values = c("Naive" = "#D73027", "LTMLE" = "#4575B4",
                                    "LMTP-static" = "#74ADD1",
                                    "LMTP-shift" = "#1A9850")) +
      labs(x = "Adherence Level / Policy",
           y = "Bias in Risk Difference (Drug A − Drug B)",
           title = "Monte Carlo RD Bias Comparison",
           fill = "Estimator") +
      theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
      theme(legend.position = "bottom", panel.grid.major.x = element_blank())
  }
}
Show code 
if (!is.null(mc_summary)) {
  rr_rows <- mc_summary[grepl(" RR", mc_summary$estimand), ]
  if (nrow(rr_rows) > 0 && any(!is.na(rr_rows$bias_median))) {
    rr_rows$estimator <- ifelse(grepl("Naive", rr_rows$estimand), "Naive",
                          ifelse(grepl("LTMLE", rr_rows$estimand), "LTMLE",
                          ifelse(grepl("LMTP-static", rr_rows$estimand), "LMTP-static",
                                 "LMTP-shift")))
    rr_rows$adherence <- ifelse(grepl("low", rr_rows$estimand), "low",
                          ifelse(grepl("medium", rr_rows$estimand), "medium",
                          ifelse(grepl("high", rr_rows$estimand), "high",
                          ifelse(grepl("z=1", rr_rows$estimand), "low",
                          ifelse(grepl("z=2", rr_rows$estimand), "medium",
                          ifelse(grepl("z=3", rr_rows$estimand), "high", "shift"))))))
    rr_rows$adherence <- factor(rr_rows$adherence,
                                 levels = c("low", "medium", "high", "shift"))

    ggplot(rr_rows[!is.na(rr_rows$bias_median), ],
           aes(x = adherence, y = bias_median, fill = estimator)) +
      geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.7), width = 0.6, alpha = 0.85) +
      geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40") +
      scale_fill_manual(values = c("Naive" = "#D73027", "LTMLE" = "#4575B4",
                                    "LMTP-static" = "#74ADD1",
                                    "LMTP-shift" = "#1A9850")) +
      labs(x = "Adherence Level / Policy",
           y = "Median Bias in Risk Ratio (Drug A / Drug B)",
           title = "Monte Carlo RR Median-Bias Comparison",
           fill = "Estimator") +
      theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
      theme(legend.position = "bottom", panel.grid.major.x = element_blank())
  }
}

9.7 Interpretive discussion

The results from the 500-replication Monte Carlo study highlight four key patterns.

1. Naive RD estimates show moderate bias in this DGP, but are unreliable for the wrong reasons. Under this DGP — strong health-to-adherence feedback (\(\beta_{Z \leftarrow L} = 0.80\)) with no explicit cumulative-history term (\(\beta_{Y \leftarrow \bar{Z}} = 0\); history captured implicitly through the \(L_t\) AR(1) process) — naive RD bias at low adherence is approximately 0.035, with coverage of 72%. Naive RR estimates are more severely distorted: at low adherence, the naive RR has bias of approximately 0.151 on the ratio scale, and coverage is only 32%. This apparent partial performance of naive RD arises because the feedback structure creates partial cancellation of biases within adherence strata — a structural artifact of this specific DGP, not a general property. The degree of naive RD bias varies with parameterization; stronger forgiveness differentials or additional unmeasured pathways would produce larger naive bias.

2. LTMLE eliminates confounding bias at all adherence levels, but pays a variance cost at low adherence. LTMLE RD bias is near-zero across all three static-regime adherence levels: approximately 0.008 at \(z = 1\), 0.0012 at \(z = 2\), and 0.0029 at \(z = 3\), compared to truth of approximately -0.114 at \(z = 1\). In contrast, naive RD bias is approximately 0.035 — attenuating toward the null — confirming that time-dependent confounding overestimates the similarity between regimens under naive analysis. The tradeoff for LTMLE’s near-zero bias is variance: the influence-curve-based variance estimator is conservative at low adherence (SE/SD = 1.66 at \(z = 1\)), yielding 100% empirical coverage but only 16% power at \(z = 1\), compared to 89% power for naive. The near-nominal LTMLE coverage at \(z = 1\) therefore reflects honest acknowledgment of positivity strain: the point estimate is unbiased but the CIs are wide, correctly signaling that this causal parameter is weakly identified in these regions of the data. Coverage at \(z = 2\) and \(z = 3\) is 98% and 100% respectively, reflecting conservative but honest inference where positivity is less severe. The RR is extremely unstable at \(z = 2\) and \(z = 3\), where risks approaching zero cause division to amplify estimation noise in sparse-support strata.

3. LMTP-static suffers density-ratio collapse. SE/SD ratios fall as low as 0.071 at \(z = 2\), with only 11% empirical coverage. This behavior reflects extreme variability in estimated density ratios when the intervention places substantial mass in regions of near-zero observed treatment density. This illustrates that importance-weighted estimators can fail wherever static-regime trajectories are thinly supported relative to observed data, and confirms that LMTP-static should not be used as a default robustness check in settings where static adherence regimes are rare.

4. Risk difference is the stable contrast; RR is unreliable whenever risks are extreme. The RD is bounded and interpretable across all estimators and adherence levels, with LMTP-shift achieving the best calibration (SE/SD = 1.05, coverage = 97%). Reporting RD as the primary contrast is particularly important when any stratum approaches either risk extreme.

9.8 Secondary treatment-comparison results

As a secondary analysis, we compared regimens under a shared adherence distribution by mapping both treatment strategies to the observed adherence pattern of Drug A (the more forgiving regimen). This controlled-adherence contrast isolates differences in regimen-specific outcome risk apart from differences in adherence behavior attributable to side effects or other regimen-specific pathways. The resulting risk difference therefore reflects regimen differences under a common adherence pattern, not differences arising through each regimen’s natural adherence behavior. Full results from this secondary analysis are reported in Section 13.10.

9.9 V3 EHR Monte Carlo results (continuous PDC)

Show code 
if (!mc_v3_available) {
  no_results_msg("V3 EHR Monte Carlo results. Run scripts/run_monte_carlo_ehr.R to generate")
}

Pre-computed results not found. V3 EHR Monte Carlo results. Run scripts/run_monte_carlo_ehr.R to generate requires cached outputs. Re-render with caching enabled.

Show code 
if (!is.null(mc_v3$shift_dt) && nrow(mc_v3$shift_dt) > 0) {
  shift_plot_dt <- copy(mc_v3$shift_dt)
  shift_plot_dt[, Drug := ifelse(A0 == 1, "Drug A (INSTI)", "Drug B (NNRTI)")]

  ggplot(shift_plot_dt, aes(x = RD, fill = Drug)) +
    geom_histogram(bins = 40, alpha = 0.7, position = "identity") +
    geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40") +
    scale_fill_manual(values = drug_pal_v3) +
    facet_wrap(~Drug, ncol = 2) +
    labs(x = "Risk Difference (shifted - observed)",
         y = "Replications",
         title = "MC Distribution of LMTP-Shift RD (+10pp PDC)") +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
    theme(legend.position = "none")
}
Show code 
if (!is.null(mc_v3$qmap_dt) && nrow(mc_v3$qmap_dt) > 0) {
  qmap_long <- melt(mc_v3$qmap_dt,
    measure.vars = c("adherence_mediated", "residual_pharmacologic"),
    variable.name = "Component", value.name = "RD")
  qmap_long[, Component := fifelse(Component == "adherence_mediated",
    "Adherence-mediated", "Residual pharmacologic")]

  ggplot(qmap_long, aes(x = RD, fill = Component)) +
    geom_histogram(bins = 40, alpha = 0.7, position = "identity") +
    geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40") +
    scale_fill_manual(values = c("Adherence-mediated" = "#E66100",
                                  "Residual pharmacologic" = "#4575B4")) +
    labs(x = "Risk Difference Component",
         y = "Replications",
         title = "Quantile-Mapping Decomposition Across MC Replications") +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
    theme(legend.position = "bottom")
}
Show code 
if (!is.null(mc_v3$shift_summary) && !is.null(mc_v3$naive_summary)) {
  se_cal <- data.table(
    Estimator = c(
      paste0("LMTP-shift (Drug ", c("A", "B"), ")"),
      if (!is.null(mc_v3$qmap_summary)) "LMTP-qmap" else NULL,
      paste0("Naive (", mc_v3$naive_summary$adh_cat, ")")),
    SE_SD = c(
      mc_v3$shift_summary$se_ratio,
      if (!is.null(mc_v3$qmap_summary)) mc_v3$qmap_summary$mean_SE / mc_v3$qmap_summary$sd_RD else NULL,
      mc_v3$naive_summary$mean_SE / mc_v3$naive_summary$sd_RD))

  ggplot(se_cal, aes(x = reorder(Estimator, SE_SD), y = SE_SD)) +
    geom_col(fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.8) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", color = "red", linewidth = 0.8) +
    coord_flip() +
    labs(x = NULL, y = "SE / SD Ratio (1.0 = perfect calibration)",
         title = "Standard Error Calibration (V3 EHR MC)") +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
}
LMTP-static with continuous PDC

LMTP-static failed on all replications when targeting exact continuous PDC values. Static regimes set PDC to a point mass (e.g., 0.75) at every time point, but the observed continuous PDC has zero probability at any exact value, creating a complete positivity violation. This motivates two solutions: (1) categorize PDC into ordinal bins before running LMTP-static (as done in the single-cohort V3 EHR analysis), or (2) use shift or quantile-mapping estimators that respect the support of the observed treatment distribution. Results using categorized adherence are pending re-run.

10 Results Under the Realistic EHR-Style DGP

The analyses above use a simplified ordinal-adherence DGP to isolate the core methodological issues. This section presents parallel results under a more realistic EHR-style DGP that simulates raw claims and EMR tables with continuous PDC, informative monitoring, informative censoring, informative treatment switching, and 36 months of follow-up in 12 quarterly blocks. In this DGP, Drug A and Drug B have the same adherence distribution (no adherence advantage for either drug), but Drug A has a stronger pharmacologic forgiveness advantage: Drug A’s baseline failure penalty is larger (fail_beta_A = -0.25) and the forgiveness interaction with suboptimal adherence is stronger (fail_beta_A_adh = -0.80), so the entire drug difference operates through the pharmacologic pathway rather than through adherence behaviour. Continuous PDC is categorised into four ordinal bins (<65%, 65–80%, 80–90%, >90%) for the naive estimator and for LMTP-static estimation to avoid positivity violations with continuous treatment.

Show code 
if (!v3_available) {
  no_results_msg("V3 EHR results. Run scripts/save_v3_results_for_tutorial.R to generate")
}

10.1 V3 EHR cohort summary

Show code 
v3_summary <- data.table(
  Feature = c("Sample size", "Follow-up", "Block size", "Initial INSTI (%)",
              "Mean PDC", "Ever censored (%)", "Ever switched (%)",
              "Cumulative failure (%)", "Cumulative death (%)"),
  Value = c(
    sprintf("%d", length(unique(panel_v3$patient_id))),
    "36 months", "90 days (quarterly)",
    sprintf("%.1f", mean(panel_v3[block == 0]$A0) * 100),
    sprintf("%.2f", mean(panel_v3$PDC, na.rm = TRUE)),
    sprintf("%.1f", mean(panel_v3[, max(C), by = patient_id]$V1) * 100),
    sprintf("%.1f", mean(panel_v3[, max(S), by = patient_id]$V1) * 100),
    sprintf("%.1f", mean(panel_v3[, max(Y), by = patient_id]$V1) * 100),
    sprintf("%.1f", mean(panel_v3[, max(D), by = patient_id]$V1) * 100)))

kable(v3_summary, caption = "V3 EHR simulated cohort characteristics") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped"), full_width = FALSE)

10.2 V3 oracle forgiveness curves

Show code

  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) + geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent, breaks = seq(0.3, 1, 0.1)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  scale_color_manual(values = drug_pal_v3) +

       color = "Regimen", title = "Oracle Forgiveness Curves (V3 EHR DGP)") +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

oracle_v3_wide <- dcast(oracle_v3, adh_target ~ drug, value.var = "cum_risk")
setnames(oracle_v3_wide, c("PDC", "Drug A", "Drug B"))
oracle_v3_wide[, RD := `Drug A` - `Drug B`]
kable(oracle_v3_wide[PDC %in% seq(0.3, 1.0, 0.1)], digits = 3,
      caption = "V3 EHR oracle risks and risk differences at selected PDC levels") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)

10.3 Informative process diagnostics

Show code 
mon_by_adh_v3 <- panel_v3[C == 0 & D == 0, .(
  mon_rate = mean(M, na.rm = TRUE), n = .N
), by = .(block, adh_cat = cut(PDC, breaks = c(-0.01, 0.65, 0.80, 0.90, 1.01),
                                 labels = c("<65%", "65-80%", "80-90%", ">90%")))]

ggplot(mon_by_adh_v3, aes(x = block, y = mon_rate, color = adh_cat)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) + geom_point(size = 2) +

       title = "V3 EHR: Monitoring Rate by Adherence") +

), by = .(block, adh_cat = cut(PDC, breaks = c(-0.01, 0.65, 0.80, 0.90, 1.01),
                                 labels = c("<65%", "65-80%", "80-90%", ">90%")))]

ggplot(cens_rate_v3, aes(x = block, y = cum_cens, color = adh_cat)) +

), by = .(block, A0 = ifelse(A0 == 1, "Drug A", "Drug B"))]

10.5 LMTP-static estimates vs oracle

Show code 
if (!is.null(lmtp_static_v3) && nrow(lmtp_static_v3) > 0) {
  lmtp_static_v3[, drug_full := ifelse(Drug == "Drug A",
                                        "Drug A (INSTI)", "Drug B (NNRTI)")]
  ggplot() +
    geom_line(data = oracle_v3, aes(x = adh_target, y = cum_risk, color = drug),
              linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.5) +

               aes(x = PDC, y = Estimate, color = drug_full), size = 3) +

                  aes(x = PDC, ymin = Estimate - SE, ymax = Estimate + SE,
                      color = drug_full), width = 0.015) +

         color = "Regimen",
         title = "V3 EHR: Estimated vs Oracle Forgiveness Curves") +

}

10.6 LMTP-shift results (+10pp PDC)

Show code 
if (!is.null(lmtp_shift_v3) && nrow(lmtp_shift_v3) > 0) {
  kable(lmtp_shift_v3, digits = 3,
        caption = "V3 EHR: LMTP shift results — effect of +10pp PDC increase by drug") |>

10.7 Quantile-mapping drug comparison

Show code 
if (!is.null(qmap_v3)) {
  qmap_display <- data.table(
    Estimand = c("Drug A, observed adherence", "Drug B, observed adherence",
      "Drug B, Drug A adherence (q-map)",
      "Effect of adherence shift on Drug B",
      "Residual drug difference (Drug A obs - Drug B shifted)"),
    Risk_or_RD = c(qmap_v3$risk_A_obs, qmap_v3$risk_B_obs, qmap_v3$risk_B_shifted,
      qmap_v3$RD_adherence_shift, qmap_v3$residual_pharmacologic),
    Interpretation = c("Failure risk under natural Drug A adherence",
      "Failure risk under natural Drug B adherence",
      "Failure risk if Drug B patients had Drug A adherence (same distribution in this DGP)",
      "Adherence-mediated component (near zero: both drugs have same adherence distribution)",
      "Residual pharmacologic forgiveness difference (dominates when adherence distributions overlap)"))

  kable(qmap_display, digits = 3,
        caption = "V3 EHR: Decomposing Drug A vs Drug B via quantile-mapped adherence (both drugs have the same adherence distribution in this DGP)") |>
    kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
}

10.8 Quantile-based adherence shift intervention

Show code 
# ── Node structure for LMTP on V3 EHR data ──────────────────────────
K_v3 <- length(grep("^PDC_", names(wide_v3)))
trt_nodes_v3 <- paste0("PDC_", 0:(K_v3 - 1))
outcome_nodes_v3 <- paste0("Y_", 0:(K_v3 - 1))
cens_nodes_v3 <- paste0("C_", 0:(K_v3 - 1))

baseline_v3 <- c("sex", "age_at_index", "msm", "cci",
                  "race_black", "race_hispanic",
                  "region_south", "region_midwest", "region_west", "A0")
baseline_nodrug_v3 <- setdiff(baseline_v3, "A0")

time_vary_v3 <- lapply(0:(K_v3 - 1), function(k) {
  tv <- c(paste0("VL_log10_locf_", k), paste0("CD4_locf_", k))
  for (extra in c("depression_", "substance_use_", "S_", "M_", "D_")) {
    col <- paste0(extra, k)
    if (col %in% names(wide_v3)) tv <- c(tv, col)
  }
  tv
})

baseline_cov_v3 <- c("age_at_index", "sex", "msm", "cci",
                      "race_black", "race_hispanic")
sl_lib_v3 <- c("SL.glm", "SL.mean")
Show code 
# Fit conditional quantile regressions for Drug A and Drug B
# at each time block and build the shift function
qmap_v3_detail <- load_cached("qmap_v3_ehr_detail", quote({
  library(quantreg)
  tau_grid_v3 <- seq(0.05, 0.95, by = 0.05)
  dt_qmap_v3 <- as.data.frame(copy(wide_v3))

  cqr_models_v3 <- list()
  for (k in 0:(K_v3 - 1)) {
    pdc_col <- paste0("PDC_", k)
    if (k == 0) {
      cov_cols <- baseline_cov_v3
    } else {
      cov_cols <- c(baseline_cov_v3,
                    paste0("VL_log10_locf_", k),
                    paste0("CD4_locf_", k),
                    paste0("PDC_", k - 1))
    }
    fmla <- as.formula(paste(pdc_col, "~", paste(cov_cols, collapse = " + ")))
    dat_A <- dt_qmap_v3[dt_qmap_v3$A0 == 1 & !is.na(dt_qmap_v3[[pdc_col]]), ]
    dat_B <- dt_qmap_v3[dt_qmap_v3$A0 == 0 & !is.na(dt_qmap_v3[[pdc_col]]), ]

    fits_A <- lapply(tau_grid_v3, function(tau) suppressWarnings(rq(fmla, tau = tau, data = dat_A)))
    fits_B <- lapply(tau_grid_v3, function(tau) suppressWarnings(rq(fmla, tau = tau, data = dat_B)))
    cqr_models_v3[[k + 1]] <- list(fits_A = fits_A, fits_B = fits_B)
  }

  # Shift function: map Drug B adherence to Drug A distribution
  shift_qmap_v3 <- function(data, trt) {
    k <- as.integer(gsub("PDC_", "", trt))
    a_obs <- data[[trt]]
    a_shifted <- a_obs
    is_b <- data$A0 == 0
    if (any(is_b)) {
      mods <- cqr_models_v3[[k + 1]]
      qmat_B <- sapply(mods$fits_B, function(fit) suppressWarnings(predict(fit, newdata = data[is_b, ])))
      qmat_A <- sapply(mods$fits_A, function(fit) suppressWarnings(predict(fit, newdata = data[is_b, ])))
      for (i in seq_len(nrow(qmat_B))) { qmat_B[i, ] <- sort(qmat_B[i, ]); qmat_A[i, ] <- sort(qmat_A[i, ]) }

      find_tau_v3 <- function(a_obs, qmat) {
        n <- length(a_obs); tau_out <- numeric(n)
        for (i in seq_len(n)) {
          qs <- qmat[i, ]
          if (a_obs[i] <= qs[1]) tau_out[i] <- tau_grid_v3[1]
          else if (a_obs[i] >= qs[length(qs)]) tau_out[i] <- tau_grid_v3[length(tau_grid_v3)]
          else tau_out[i] <- approx(qs, tau_grid_v3, xout = a_obs[i], rule = 2)$y
        }
        tau_out
      }

      tau_star <- find_tau_v3(a_obs[is_b], qmat_B)
      for (i in seq_len(sum(is_b))) {
        a_shifted[which(is_b)[i]] <- approx(tau_grid_v3, qmat_A[i, ], xout = tau_star[i], rule = 2)$y
      }
      a_shifted[is_b] <- pmax(0.01, pmin(0.99, a_shifted[is_b]))
    }
    a_shifted
  }

  # Run LMTP: Drug B observed vs shifted, Drug A observed
  sub_B <- dt_qmap_v3[dt_qmap_v3$A0 == 0, ]
  sub_A <- dt_qmap_v3[dt_qmap_v3$A0 == 1, ]

  fit_B_obs <- lmtp::lmtp_sdr(
    data = sub_B, trt = trt_nodes_v3, outcome = outcome_nodes_v3,
    baseline = baseline_nodrug_v3, time_vary = time_vary_v3, cens = cens_nodes_v3,
    shift = NULL, mtp = FALSE, outcome_type = "survival",
    learners_outcome = sl_lib_v3, learners_trt = sl_lib_v3, folds = 5)

  fit_B_shifted <- lmtp::lmtp_sdr(
    data = sub_B, trt = trt_nodes_v3, outcome = outcome_nodes_v3,
    baseline = baseline_nodrug_v3, time_vary = time_vary_v3, cens = cens_nodes_v3,
    shift = shift_qmap_v3, mtp = TRUE, outcome_type = "survival",
    learners_outcome = sl_lib_v3, learners_trt = sl_lib_v3, folds = 5)

  fit_A_obs <- lmtp::lmtp_sdr(
    data = sub_A, trt = trt_nodes_v3, outcome = outcome_nodes_v3,
    baseline = baseline_nodrug_v3, time_vary = time_vary_v3, cens = cens_nodes_v3,
    shift = NULL, mtp = FALSE, outcome_type = "survival",
    learners_outcome = sl_lib_v3, learners_trt = sl_lib_v3, folds = 5)

  contrast_qmap <- lmtp::lmtp_contrast(fit_B_shifted, ref = fit_B_obs, type = "additive")

  # Also capture the shifted adherence distributions for visualization
  shift_viz_blocks <- c(0, floor(K_v3/4), floor(K_v3/2), K_v3 - 1)
  viz_list <- lapply(shift_viz_blocks, function(k) {
    pdc_col <- paste0("PDC_", k)
    dat_k <- dt_qmap_v3[!is.na(dt_qmap_v3[[pdc_col]]), ]
    shifted_vals <- shift_qmap_v3(dat_k, pdc_col)
    data.table(
      block = k,
      PDC = c(dat_k[[pdc_col]][dat_k$A0 == 0],
              shifted_vals[dat_k$A0 == 0],
              dat_k[[pdc_col]][dat_k$A0 == 1]),
      Source = c(rep("Drug B observed", sum(dat_k$A0 == 0)),
                 rep("Drug B shifted", sum(dat_k$A0 == 0)),
                 rep("Drug A observed", sum(dat_k$A0 == 1))))
  })
  shift_viz_dt <- rbindlist(viz_list)

  list(
    risk_A_obs     = 1 - fit_A_obs$estimate@x,
    risk_B_obs     = 1 - fit_B_obs$estimate@x,
    risk_B_shifted = 1 - fit_B_shifted$estimate@x,
    RD_shift       = contrast_qmap$estimates$estimate,
    RD_se          = contrast_qmap$estimates$std.error,
    RD_ci_lo       = contrast_qmap$estimates$conf.low,
    RD_ci_hi       = contrast_qmap$estimates$conf.high,
    adherence_mediated     = (1 - fit_B_obs$estimate@x) - (1 - fit_B_shifted$estimate@x),
    residual_pharmacologic = (1 - fit_A_obs$estimate@x) - (1 - fit_B_shifted$estimate@x),
    shift_viz = shift_viz_dt)
}))
Show code 
if (!is.null(qmap_v3_detail)) {
  decomp_tbl <- data.table(
    Component = c(
      "Drug A risk (observed adherence)",
      "Drug B risk (observed adherence)",
      "Drug B risk (shifted to Drug A adherence)",
      "Adherence-mediated (Drug B obs - Drug B shifted)",
      "Residual pharmacologic (Drug A obs - Drug B shifted)",
      "Quantile-map shift RD (95% CI)"),
    Estimate = c(
      sprintf("%.3f", qmap_v3_detail$risk_A_obs),
      sprintf("%.3f", qmap_v3_detail$risk_B_obs),
      sprintf("%.3f", qmap_v3_detail$risk_B_shifted),
      sprintf("%+.4f", qmap_v3_detail$adherence_mediated),
      sprintf("%+.4f", qmap_v3_detail$residual_pharmacologic),
      sprintf("%+.4f (%.4f, %.4f)", qmap_v3_detail$RD_shift,
              qmap_v3_detail$RD_ci_lo, qmap_v3_detail$RD_ci_hi)))

  kable(decomp_tbl, caption = "Quantile-mapping decomposition: adherence-mediated vs residual pharmacologic") |>
    kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
}
Show code 
if (!is.null(qmap_v3_detail) && !is.null(qmap_v3_detail$shift_viz)) {
  shift_pal <- c("Drug A observed" = "#2166AC",
                  "Drug B observed" = "#B2182B",
                  "Drug B shifted"  = "#E66100")

  ggplot(qmap_v3_detail$shift_viz, aes(x = PDC, fill = Source)) +
    geom_histogram(bins = 30, alpha = 0.6, position = "identity") +
    facet_wrap(~paste("Block", block), nrow = 2) +
    scale_fill_manual(values = shift_pal) +
    scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
    labs(x = "Adherence (PDC)", y = "Count",
         fill = "Distribution",
         title = "Adherence Distribution: Observed vs Quantile-Mapped") +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    theme(legend.position = "bottom")
}

10.9 Sensitivity: treatment-policy vs censor-at-switch

Show code 
if (!is.null(lmtp_shift_v3) && !is.null(cas_v3)) {
  tp <- copy(lmtp_shift_v3)
  tp[, Strategy := "Treatment-policy"]
  comparison <- rbind(
    tp[, .(Drug, Strategy, risk_natural, risk_shift, RD, RD_se, RD_ci_lo, RD_ci_hi)],
    cas_v3[, .(Drug, Strategy, risk_natural, risk_shift, RD, RD_se, RD_ci_lo, RD_ci_hi)])

  kable(comparison, digits = 3,
        caption = "V3 EHR: Treatment-policy vs censor-at-switch sensitivity") |>
    kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
}

10.10 Positivity: observed PDC distribution

Show code 
pdc_long_v3 <- panel_v3[C == 0 & D == 0, .(PDC, block)]
ggplot(pdc_long_v3, aes(x = PDC)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(0.65, 0.80, 0.90),
             linetype = "dashed", color = "red", linewidth = 0.7) +
  facet_wrap(~paste("Block", block), nrow = 3) +
  labs(x = "Observed PDC", y = "Count",
       title = "V3 EHR: PDC Distribution with Adherence Category Boundaries") +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1))

10.11 IPMW sensitivity for monitoring

Show code 
if (!is.null(ipmw_v3)) {
  monitored_v3 <- ipmw_v3$panel_weighted[M == 1]
  ggplot(monitored_v3, aes(x = ipmw_stab)) +
    geom_histogram(bins = 50, fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.7) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
    labs(x = "Stabilized IPMW", y = "Count",
         title = "V3 EHR: Monitoring Weight Distribution") +
    coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, quantile(monitored_v3$ipmw_stab, 0.99)))
}

11 Discussion

11.1 Summary and interpretation

Taken together, the results show that naive adherence-stratified analyses can overstate the apparent adherence–response relationship by conflating adherence with evolving health status. Longitudinal causal methods recover the intended adherence–response curve where the observed data provide adequate support. At adherence extremes, however, uncertainty increases substantially, and estimates become sensitive to limited support in the data.

Although the main text focuses on regimen-specific forgiveness as an adherence-response function, the same longitudinal intervention framework can also support comparative regimen questions. In particular, regimens may be compared under their natural adherence patterns or under a common adherence intervention, as illustrated here through a controlled-adherence secondary analysis. Informative monitoring, switching, and discontinuation introduce additional intercurrent longitudinal processes that are central in real-world HIV data; because they extend beyond the primary didactic objective of the present paper, we treat them as appendix-level methodological extensions rather than redesigning the main analysis around them.

This tutorial illustrates that regimen forgiveness is a causal adherence–response relationship defined by counterfactual outcomes under sustained adherence interventions, not an observable feature of adherence-stratified data. Because adherence and clinical status evolve jointly over time, naive comparisons across observed adherence levels do not recover this quantity. In the simulation, the bias structure depends on the relative strength of the confounding pathways: even though this DGP has strong health-to-adherence feedback (\(Z\_\beta\_L = 0.80\)) with adherence history encoded implicitly through the \(L_t\) AR(1) process rather than an explicit cumulative-adherence term (\(Y\_\beta\_{\bar{Z}} = 0\)), naive RD estimates show moderate bias at low adherence (overstating the drug difference, with naive RD approximately −0.04 pp beyond truth), while naive RR estimates and inference at extreme risk levels are more severely distorted. This DGP-dependence of naive performance is itself a key lesson — the degree of bias varies with parameterization and does not validate naive analysis in other settings.

In contrast, longitudinal g-methods recover the target parameter when their assumptions hold. LTMLE produces near-unbiased estimates at medium and high adherence, but performs differently at \(z = 1\) where the static regime is weakly supported: at low adherence where the static regime is weakly supported, positivity constraints inflate LTMLE’s variance estimator (SE/SD \(\approx\) 1.66 at \(z = 1\)), yielding conservative coverage at the cost of near-zero power. The point estimate remains near-unbiased even at \(z = 1\), but the CIs are too wide to be informative at \(n = 5{,}000\). This behaviour — near-zero bias with honest but wide uncertainty quantification — is LTMLE’s correct response to a causal question the data cannot adequately support. The influence-curve-based variance estimator is also materially conservative at medium and high adherence: nominal 95% CIs are functioning approximately as 98% intervals at \(z = 2\) and 100% at \(z = 3\). This conservatism affects interpretation of formal hypothesis tests: p-values will be inflated relative to what would be expected from a well-calibrated estimator. LMTP-static provides an alternative route to the same estimand but exhibits unstable inference when adherence trajectories are weakly represented in the data, particularly at medium and high adherence where density ratios become extreme. The LMTP shift estimator targets a different, feasible-policy estimand and shows the most stable finite-sample performance, with well-calibrated standard errors (SE/SD \(\approx\) 1.05) and minimal bias. These results reinforce two points: first, valid estimation of forgiveness requires longitudinal adjustment for time-dependent confounding; and second, the choice of estimand must be aligned with the empirical support of the data.

Although the primary analysis is framed in terms of daily oral ART with categorical adherence, the underlying framework is more general. The key object is the intervention on a time-varying exposure, not the specific adherence metric. For long-acting injectable regimens, the adherence construct shifts from daily execution to injection timing or delay, but the causal estimand, identification assumptions, and estimation strategies remain unchanged. The injectable extension illustrates this mapping using a delay-based adherence process (see Section 13.9.1 and Section 13.9), showing how forgiveness curves can take a different functional form, with a flat region followed by a sharp increase in risk once delays exceed a pharmacologically meaningful threshold.

11.2 Choosing estimands and methods

The results highlight the importance of matching the estimand to the scientific question and the data structure. Static adherence regimes provide the most direct representation of the forgiveness curve and are appropriate when support is adequate across adherence trajectories. When support is limited, however, these estimands become sensitive to extrapolation, and feasible-policy estimands based on modified treatment policies offer a more stable and interpretable alternative (Díaz, Williams, et al. 2021, 2023). Continuous adherence measures, such as PDC, further expand the set of meaningful interventions by allowing incremental shifts rather than discrete categories. In practice, static-regime and shift-policy analyses are best viewed as complementary: the former defines the theoretical adherence–response curve, while the latter answers policy-relevant questions about achievable improvements.

For the main forgiveness-curve analysis, the risk difference is the most stable contrast measure, especially when one or both risks approach the boundary near zero, because division amplifies estimation noise and risk ratios become unstable. A marginal structural model can still be useful as a secondary summary, providing a compact description of the adherence–response gradient and the regimen-by-adherence interaction, but it should be interpreted as a projection of the estimated causal curve rather than as a substitute for the nonparametric target parameter (Rosenblum and Laan 2010; Petersen et al. 2014).

11.3 Scope and extensions

The primary focus of this tutorial is the estimation of forgiveness curves under time-varying adherence in a simplified oral-ART setting. Additional components, including delivery-system comparisons, informative monitoring, censoring, and treatment switching, are presented as extensions to illustrate how the same causal framework generalises to more complex real-world data structures. These extensions are not part of the primary Monte Carlo evaluation but instead show how the node structure, estimands, and estimation strategy can be adapted when the data-generating process includes additional sources of bias or missingness (see Section 13.10).

In particular, the delivery-system extension highlights how differences between oral and injectable regimens can be decomposed into execution-related and residual effects. This reflects a broader shift in modern ART research, where differences between regimens increasingly arise from adherence processes and delivery mechanisms rather than efficacy under perfect adherence. The framework presented here provides a unified way to formalise and estimate these comparisons.

11.4 Stylization and limitations

Several simplifications and limitations should be noted explicitly. First, adherence is represented using a simplified three-level ordinal variable (\(Z_t \in \{1, 2, 3\}\)) rather than continuous PDC; this reduces computational complexity and illustrates the core methods, but may obscure finer-grained variation. Continuous PDC, as discussed in Section 6.5, is more flexible but requires density-ratio estimation for the LMTP machinery. Second, the primary analysis assumes complete outcome ascertainment: every patient’s virologic failure status is observed at each block without informative monitoring. Real applications require handling the masking of failures in unmonitored periods, and this complication is discussed in Section 13.10.2 (appendix extension). Third, drug assignment is randomised 1:1 in the simulation; real observational settings require additional baseline confounding adjustment via propensity weighting or other methods, in addition to the longitudinal adjustment that is the primary focus here.

Fourth, the treatment-confounder feedback structure and the forgiveness differential are intentionally visible for pedagogic clarity. The low-adherence risk difference between drugs (approximately -0.11 on the risk-difference scale) remains somewhat stylized compared to modern INSTI-versus-comparator settings, where pharmacologic differences at suboptimal adherence are more modest. The DGP was revised in V13 to substantially reduce earlier over-amplification of failure rates at low adherence, while retaining a visible forgiveness differential sufficient for pedagogic demonstration. Fifth, the bias of the naive estimator depends on DGP parameterization: this paper illustrates one configuration where naive RD bias is moderate and visible at low adherence, driven by strong health-to-adherence feedback (\(\beta_{Z \leftarrow L} = 0.80\)) with adherence history encoded implicitly through the \(L_t\) AR(1) process (\(\beta_{Y \leftarrow \bar{Z}} = 0\)). Naive RR bias is more severe. In configurations with larger forgiveness differentials, additional unmeasured confounders, or different adherence distributions, naive RD bias could be substantially larger.

Beyond these DGP-specific simplifications, several general limitations apply. The implementation uses logistic regression (SL.glm) as the sole nuisance model for transparency; applied analyses should use richer SuperLearner libraries. Finite-sample variability remains substantial when adherence trajectories are weakly supported, and cross-validated TMLE is one possible extension for improving finite-sample performance (Zheng and Laan 2011). The injectable extension is illustrative rather than pharmacokinetically calibrated; real applications would require delay distributions and hazard models informed by empirical pharmacokinetic studies.

These simplifications were deliberate. They allow the tutorial to focus on the core statistical problem — the distinction between associational and interventional estimands under treatment-confounder feedback — while showing in the appendix how the same framework extends to more realistic observational settings. This DGP is intended to reflect the qualitative structure of modern HIV care; future work should calibrate these mechanisms to specific cohort or trial data. Among these extensions, incorporating informative monitoring and applying the framework to long-acting injectable therapies are likely to have the greatest immediate impact in applied HIV research.

11.5 Sensitivity to unmeasured confounding

Identification of the causal estimand relies on sequential exchangeability, which is unlikely to hold exactly in observational HIV data. Important drivers of adherence and outcomes, such as mental health, housing instability, and substance use, are often incompletely measured. When these factors are unobserved, estimated forgiveness curves may be biased. Sensitivity analyses are therefore essential in applied work. E-values provide a simple summary of robustness to unmeasured confounding (VanderWeele and Ding 2017), while longitudinal bounding approaches can account for the accumulation of bias over time (Robins and Richardson 2010). Negative control outcomes offer an empirical check: if an adherence effect is detected for an outcome that adherence cannot plausibly influence, residual confounding is likely present.

These sensitivity analyses are not implemented in the current tutorial because the simulation satisfies sequential exchangeability by construction. In real-world applications, however, they should be considered part of a minimum diagnostic framework for interpreting estimated forgiveness curves.

11.6 Practical implications for applied analyses

For applied analysts, the main practical lesson is that positivity should be assessed before choosing an estimator. When sustained adherence regimes have adequate support, LTMLE provides a direct route to the target forgiveness curve. When support is weak, LMTP shift interventions offer a more stable alternative that remains clinically interpretable. This distinction is especially important in real-world HIV data, where adherence trajectories are heterogeneous and persistent high or low adherence paths may be rare.

More broadly, the relevant scientific question should determine the estimand. If the goal is to compare regimen robustness under sustained adherence levels, the forgiveness curve is the appropriate target. If the goal is to evaluate the likely impact of adherence improvement programmes, feasible-policy estimands based on LMTP are often more relevant. The same logic extends beyond daily oral ART to treatment settings in which execution is replaced by attendance, persistence, or timing, including long-acting injectable regimens.

Practical recommendations for applied analysts

Based on the Monte Carlo results in this tutorial, the following guidelines apply when choosing between estimation strategies for forgiveness curves:

  • Use LTMLE for static-regime forgiveness curves when path support for the target adherence trajectory exceeds approximately 5–10% of the observed data. LTMLE provides low bias and honest (if somewhat conservative) variance estimates under these conditions.
  • Use LMTP-shift for feasible intervention questions and when static-regime path support is thin. The shift estimator consistently achieved SE/SD \(\approx\) 1.05 and 97% empirical coverage in these simulations.
  • Do NOT use LMTP-static as a default robustness check. LMTP-static produced SE/SD as low as 0.071 at \(z = 2\), yielding only 11% empirical coverage. Relying on LMTP-static p-values for significance testing at medium or high adherence would produce severely inflated type I error rates.
  • Always inspect weight distributions and effective sample size (see Section 13.6) before interpreting static-regime estimates. Extreme cumulative weights (e.g., max weight > 1,000 or ESS/n < 0.30) indicate practical positivity strain and should trigger a shift to LMTP-shift or a sensitivity analysis with weight truncation.

12 Conclusion

Regimen forgiveness, the ability of an ART regimen to maintain viral suppression despite imperfect adherence, is a causal property of the treatment-adherence-outcome system, not an observable characteristic that can be read off from adherence-stratified outcome data. Estimating forgiveness therefore requires methods that account for the feedback between clinical status, adherence, and virologic outcomes over time.

This tutorial provides a complete workflow following the causal roadmap: from scientific question to causal estimand, to identified statistical target parameter \(\Psi(P_0)\), to targeted estimator. The primary analysis uses LTMLE to estimate static-regime forgiveness curves, with LMTP used both as a complementary feasible-policy approach and as a framework that generalises naturally to continuous adherence measures and alternative treatment modalities. The Monte Carlo study illustrates that the relationship between naive and causal estimates is DGP-dependent: even under strong health-to-adherence feedback (\(Z\_\beta\_L = 0.80\)) with no explicit cumulative-history term (\(Y\_\beta\_{\bar{Z}} = 0\); history encoded implicitly through the \(L_t\) AR(1) process), naive RD estimates show moderate but non-negligible bias driven by structural features of the feedback loop, while LTMLE and LMTP-shift recover the target parameter with better-calibrated inference when their assumptions are met.

While demonstrated here primarily with daily oral ART, the framework applies more broadly to treatment settings in which effectiveness depends on time-varying adherence or execution. For long-acting injectables, the adherence construct shifts from daily pill-taking to injection timing, but the causal structure, identification assumptions, and estimation strategies carry over directly (see Section 13.9.1 and Section 13.9 for an illustrative extension).

12.1 References

13 Appendix

13.1 ICH E9(R1) estimand attributes

For regulatory-oriented readers, the static-intervention forgiveness estimand can be expressed in terms of the five attributes specified by International Council for Harmonisation (2019):

Attribute Specification
Population Adult PLWH initiating or maintaining ART
Treatment Regimen \(A_0\) (Drug A or Drug B) under forced adherence \(\bar{Z} = \bar{z}\)
Endpoint Virologic failure (VL ≥200 copies/mL) by 48 weeks
Summary measure Cumulative incidence \(\psi(a_0, z) = P(Y_K^{a_0, \bar{z}} = 1)\)
Intercurrent event strategy Treatment-policy (adherence lapses handled via the forced-regime intervention)

13.2 Identification assumptions

13.2.1 Consistency (SUTVA)

If \(A_0 = a_0\) and \(\bar{Z}_t = \bar{z}_t\), then \(Y_t^{a_0, \bar{z}} = Y_t\). This assumes no interference between patients and a well-defined intervention.

13.2.2 Sequential exchangeability

\[Y_K^{a_0, \bar{z}} \perp\!\!\!\perp Z_t \mid \bar{L}_t, \bar{Z}_{t-1}, A_0, W \quad \forall t\]

This is the “no unmeasured confounders” assumption, conditional on the measured time-varying and baseline covariates. In real-world HIV data, sequential exchangeability may be threatened by incompletely measured behavioral and structural determinants of adherence, including mental health, stigma, unstable housing, and care disruption. As a result, valid causal interpretation depends on the extent to which available covariates adequately capture these processes.

13.2.3 Positivity

\[P(Z_t = z \mid \bar{L}_t = \bar{l}_t, \bar{Z}_{t-1} = \bar{z}_{t-1}, A_0 = a_0, W = w) > 0\]

for all \((t, z, \bar{l}_t, \bar{z}_{t-1}, a_0, w)\) in the support. This is checked empirically in Section 13.4.

13.3 Simulated cohort details

13.3.1 DGP parameters

Show code 
dgp_p <- attr(dat, "dgp_params")
param_table <- data.frame(
  Parameter = c(
    "Drug assignment probability",
    "Baseline severity prevalence",
    "Baseline depression prevalence",
    "L_t AR(1) persistence",
    "L_t effect of prior Z",
    "Z_t effect of L_t (health -> adherence)",
    "Z_t effect of drug (Drug B side effects)",
    "Z_t depression effect",
    "Y_t baseline log-odds",
    "Y_t low-adherence penalty",
    "Y_t medium-adherence penalty",
    "Y_t effect of health L_t",
    "Y_t effect of cumulative mean adherence",
    "Y_t Drug A direct effect",
    "Y_t Drug A x low-adherence forgiveness",
    "Y_t Drug A x medium-adherence forgiveness"
  ),
  Symbol = c(
    "P(A0=1)", "P(W_sev)", "P(W_dep)",
    "L_ar", "L_beta_Z", "Z_beta_L", "Z_beta_A0", "Z_beta_dep",
    "Y_int", "Y_beta_Z_low", "Y_beta_Z_med",
    "Y_beta_L", "Y_beta_Zbar",
    "Y_beta_A0", "Y_beta_forgive_low", "Y_beta_forgive_med"
  ),
  Value = c(
    dgp_p$a0_prob, dgp_p$sev_prob, dgp_p$dep_prob,
    dgp_p$L_ar, dgp_p$L_beta_Z, dgp_p$Z_beta_L, dgp_p$Z_beta_A0, dgp_p$Z_beta_dep,
    dgp_p$Y_int, dgp_p$Y_beta_Z_low, dgp_p$Y_beta_Z_med,
    dgp_p$Y_beta_L, dgp_p$Y_beta_Zbar,
    dgp_p$Y_beta_A0, dgp_p$Y_beta_forgive_low, dgp_p$Y_beta_forgive_med
  ),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)
kable(param_table, digits = 2, align = "llr") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "condensed"), full_width = FALSE,
                font_size = 11)

13.3.2 Baseline characteristics

Show code 
base_tab <- data.frame(
  Characteristic = c("N", "Drug A (more forgiving), %",
                     "Age, mean (sd)", "Low CD4 / severity, %",
                     "Depression, %", "Prior instability, %"),
  Value = c(
    nrow(dat),
    sprintf("%.1f", 100 * mean(dat$A0)),
    sprintf("%.2f (%.2f)", mean(dat$W_age), sd(dat$W_age)),
    sprintf("%.1f", 100 * mean(dat$W_sev)),
    sprintf("%.1f", 100 * mean(dat$W_dep)),
    sprintf("%.1f", 100 * mean(dat$W_instab))
  ),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)
kable(base_tab, align = "lr") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "condensed"), full_width = FALSE)

13.3.3 Adherence distribution over time

Show code 
z_long <- do.call(rbind, lapply(seq_len(K), function(t) {
  z_col <- paste0("Z_", t)
  data.frame(
    block = t,
    arm = ifelse(dat$A0 == 1, "Drug A", "Drug B"),
    adherence = factor(dat[[z_col]], levels = 1:3,
                       labels = c("Low", "Medium", "High")),
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
}))
z_long <- z_long[!is.na(z_long$adherence), ]

ggplot(z_long, aes(x = adherence, fill = adherence)) +
  geom_bar(alpha = 0.8) +
  facet_grid(arm ~ paste0("Block ", block)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Low" = "#D73027", "Medium" = "#FEE08B",
                                "High" = "#1A9850"), guide = "none") +
  labs(x = "Adherence Level", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

13.3.4 Cumulative virologic failure

Show code 
cum_fail <- data.frame(
  block = rep(0:K, 2),
  arm = rep(c("Drug A", "Drug B"), each = K + 1),
  cum_inc = c(
    0, sapply(1:K, function(t)
      mean(rowSums(dat[dat$A0 == 1, paste0("Y_", 1:t), drop = FALSE],
                   na.rm = TRUE) > 0)),
    0, sapply(1:K, function(t)
      mean(rowSums(dat[dat$A0 == 0, paste0("Y_", 1:t), drop = FALSE],
                   na.rm = TRUE) > 0))
  )
)

ggplot(cum_fail, aes(x = block, y = cum_inc, color = arm, group = arm)) +
  geom_step(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Drug A" = "#4575B4", "Drug B" = "#D73027")) +
  labs(x = "Block", y = "Cumulative Incidence", color = "Arm") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom") +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, NA))

13.3.5 Oracle counterfactual risks

Show code 
if (!is.null(oracle_truth)) {
  ot <- oracle_truth
  ot$arm <- ifelse(ot$a0 == 1, "Drug A", "Drug B")
  ot$adherence <- c("Low", "Medium", "High", "Low", "Medium", "High",
                     "Shift +1", "Shift +1")[match(ot$z_target,
                     c("1","2","3","1","2","3","shift","shift"))]
  kable(ot[, c("arm", "adherence", "cum_risk_oracle")],
        digits = 4,
        col.names = c("Arm", "Adherence Regime", "Oracle Risk"),
        align = "llr") |>
    kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "condensed"), full_width = FALSE)
}

The oracle risks confirm the built-in forgiveness mechanism: at high adherence, both drugs perform similarly; at low adherence, Drug B’s risk increases much more sharply than Drug A’s.

13.4 Positivity Diagnostics

Before estimating risks under forced adherence regimes, we check whether the data provide adequate support. Positivity, the requirement that every adherence level has positive probability given measured covariates at each time point, is the identification assumption most likely to be strained in practice, and violations are the leading cause of unreliable causal estimates in longitudinal analyses.

13.4.1 Structural versus practical positivity

Two types of positivity problems arise in practice:

  • Structural positivity violations occur when some adherence levels are impossible for certain patients by design (e.g., a patient who has been hospitalised cannot fill a prescription that month). These are intrinsic to the data structure and cannot be resolved by collecting more data.
